\chapter{Geo lift: synthetic control}
\label{ch:geo}

% Standalone-safe cross-chapter references (the did.tex \refgeo pattern): in the offprint the
% other chapters' labels do not exist, so fall back to words instead of printing "??".
\newcommand{\refiv}{\ifcsname r@ch:iv\endcsname\cref{ch:iv}\else the instrumental-variables chapter\fi}
\newcommand{\refdid}{\ifcsname r@ch:did\endcsname\cref{ch:did}\else the difference-in-differences chapter\fi}
\newcommand{\reffound}{\ifcsname r@ch:foundations\endcsname\cref{ch:foundations}\else the foundations chapter\fi}

\begin{quote}\small
\emph{A television campaign costing \keur{\nbSevenCost} ran in one of \nbSevenNDmas{} markets, with
no control group and no coin flip; the board wants a national rollout. This chapter builds what that
market would have sold anyway, a \emph{synthetic control} blended from the untreated markets, and
reads the campaign's effect off the gap. The lift is real, and it comes to \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}:
\nbSevenIroas{} euros of sales per euro spent, an apparent profit of \keur{\nbSevenApparentProfit},
and a rollout deck that writes itself. The deck is wrong. The firm keeps only its \pc{\nbSevenMargin}
gross margin, so the campaign pays only if the return beats \nbSevenBreakevenIroas{}, and the
probability it actually paid is \nbSevenPGo{}, short of the nine-in-ten a confident buy demands: the
verdict is \textbf{\nbSevenDecision}.
The chapter builds the estimate step by step, prices that break-even bet as a probability, and shows
how a single modelling choice can quietly distort it.}
\end{quote}

% =====================================================================================
\section{The decision}
\label{sec:sc:decision}

A television campaign ran in a single metropolitan area (a \emph{designated market area},
the standard unit of media buying) and nowhere else. Sales in that metro have risen since
launch. The proposal on the table is a national rollout, and the campaign that produced the
evidence cost \keur{\nbSevenCost}. The question is not whether sales rose. They did. The
question is whether the \emph{campaign} raised them, and by enough to pay for itself.

A wrong answer is expensive in both directions, and asymmetrically so. Credit the campaign
with a lift the market would have delivered anyway and the firm rolls a dud out nationally at
many multiples of the test budget. Miss a real lift and it walks away from a working channel
that a competitor will find. What makes the problem hard is stated in one sentence: \textbf{there
is no control group}. Nobody flipped a coin. The treated metro was chosen, and the rest of the
country was left dark by default.

The tempting shortcuts follow immediately, and all of them fail. A before-and-after comparison
within the treated metro books the shared trend, the season and the state of the economy as
campaign effect. A comparison against the average of the other markets books the level gap
between a large metro and an average one. Both failures are quantified in \cref{tab:sc:naive},
and both have the same root: every market in the country is riding the same waves, and the
estimator has to know which part of the treated market's rise was the wave.

What is needed is the one quantity that is never observed: the treated market's sales
\emph{had the campaign not run}. \citet{abadie2003} proposed constructing it as a weighted blend
of the untreated markets (the \emph{donor pool}) with the weights chosen so that the blend
tracks the treated market before the campaign. Suppose a mix of, say, sixty per cent of one metro
and forty per cent of two others matched the treated market week for week for forty weeks. So long
as those markets keep moving together, that same mix is the best available guess of what the
treated market would have done afterwards. The post-launch gap between the market and its
synthetic double is the estimated lift. This chapter builds that estimator, checks whether the lift
is real, and prices whether it paid. A short Bayesian layer at the end supplies the one thing the
classical tools cannot: the probability the campaign beat its cost.

% =====================================================================================
\section{Setup and notation}
\label{sec:sc:vocab}

\subsection*{The panel, the treated unit, and the donor pool}

The data is a \textbf{panel}: one row per market, one column per week, one number in each cell:
weekly sales. \Cref{tab:sc:panel} is a corner of the real thing. Exactly one market, the
\textbf{treated unit}, ran the campaign, and it did so from a known \textbf{launch week} onward.
Every other market is untreated for the whole window, and together they form the
\textbf{donor pool}: the markets from which a counterfactual will be built. There are
\nbSevenNDonors{} of them.

\input{build/tables/nb07_panel_rows}

The \textbf{pre-period} is the weeks before launch; the \textbf{post-period} is the weeks after.
The pre-period is where the method is \emph{fitted}, and the post-period is where it is
\emph{used}. That split is the whole design. The post-period is the only place the answer lives,
and the only place the method has nothing to check itself against.

\subsection*{The counterfactual is a weighted average of other markets}

Here is the one idea the chapter rests on. We cannot observe what the treated market would have
sold without the campaign, so we \emph{build} it, as a weighted blend of the donor markets:
%
\begin{equation}
  \widehat{Y}_{0,t}(0) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\, Y_{jt},
  \label{eq:sc:vocab_synth}
\end{equation}
%
one weight $w_j$ per donor, the same weights every week. (The treated market is indexed $0$ and the
donors $1, \dots, J$ throughout, matching the notebooks; the lecture deck writes the treated unit as
market $1$.) The weights are chosen so that the blend
tracks the treated market \emph{before} the campaign; they are then held fixed and carried into
the post-period. The blend is called the \textbf{synthetic control}, and the difference between
what the treated market actually did and what its synthetic double did is the \textbf{gap}:
%
\begin{equation}
  \hat\tau_t \;=\; Y_{0t} \;-\; \sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\, Y_{jt}
  \qquad\text{(the estimated lift in week } t).
  \label{eq:sc:vocab_gap}
\end{equation}
%
\Cref{tab:sc:donorcalc} performs that arithmetic once, by hand, for a single named week, so that
\cref{eq:sc:vocab_synth} stops being notation. Of the \nbSevenNDonors{} donors, only
\nbSevenDcNDonorsUsed{} carry material weight (the largest, \nbSevenDcTopName{}, at
\nbSevenDcTopWeight{}) and the rest contribute essentially nothing. Multiply each donor's sales
that week by its weight, add the column up, and the total \emph{is} the synthetic market:
\keur{\nbSevenDcSynth}. The treated market actually sold \keur{\nbSevenDcActual}. The difference,
\keur{\nbSevenDcGap}, is that week's estimated lift. That is the entire method, executed on one
week with a pencil.

\input{build/tables/nb07_donor_calc}

\begin{intuition}{Why a \emph{weighted average}, and not a regression}
The instinct of anyone trained on regression is to put the donors on the right-hand side and let
least squares choose the coefficients. That is a different estimator, and it is a worse one here,
for a reason worth internalising: unconstrained coefficients can be negative, and they can sum to
anything. A negative weight means the synthetic goes \emph{up} when a real market goes down. It
is no longer a portfolio of real places; it is an algebraic construct that happens to fit. The
weights in \cref{eq:sc:vocab_synth} are deliberately confined so that the synthetic is always a
\emph{blend of real markets}, and therefore always something a human being can point at and
defend. The next subsection makes that constraint precise, and \cref{fig:sc:simplex} prices it.
\end{intuition}

\subsection*{The simplex: interpolation, not extrapolation}

The weights are required to be non-negative and to sum to one:
%
\begin{equation}
  w \in \simplex \;=\; \Big\{\, w \;:\; w_j \ge 0 \ \ \forall j, \quad \textstyle\sum_j w_j = 1 \,\Big\}.
  \label{eq:sc:vocab_simplex}
\end{equation}
%
This set is the \textbf{unit simplex}, and the constraint is the method's defining commitment
\citep{abadie2010, abadie2021}. Geometrically it confines the synthetic to the \emph{convex hull}
of the donor pool: the synthetic can be any blend that lies \emph{between} the real markets, and
nothing that lies outside them. It \emph{interpolates}, and it is forbidden from
\emph{extrapolating}.

That constraint is not free, and \cref{fig:sc:simplex} prices it honestly rather than asserting
that the alternative collapses. Drop the constraint (fit the donors by ordinary least squares
on the pre-period) and \nbSevenUncNNegative{} of the \nbSevenNDonors{} weights go negative (the
most negative is \nbSevenUncWMin{}), and they no longer sum to one. The unconstrained fit then
tracks the pre-period \emph{better} than the simplex fit does: a root-mean-square weekly error
(RMSE; the next subsection makes it precise) of
\keur{\nbSevenRmspeUnconstrained} against \keur{\nbSevenRmspeSimplex}. It wins the weeks you can
check. And it loses the weeks you cannot. Measured out of sample against the truth, on placebo
worlds where the true lift is zero, the unconstrained fit's error is
\keur{\nbSevenHullOosUnconstrained} against the simplex fit's \keur{\nbSevenHullOosSimplex},
\nbSevenHullOosRatio$\times$ worse.

\begin{intuition}{The constraint is a promise about the object, not about the panel}
The simplex does not promise a better number on any particular panel: as \cref{fig:sc:simplex}
shows, the unconstrained fit even tracks the pre-period more closely here. What it buys is a
\emph{guarantee about what the number is}. A convex blend of real markets stays
inside the range of things that actually happened. So when it is carried twenty weeks past the
last week anyone checked, it is still doing the only thing it knows how to do: averaging real
places. An unconstrained fit, extrapolating, has no such guarantee, and the post-period is
precisely where nobody can catch it.
\end{intuition}

\input{build/floats/nb07_simplex}

\subsection*{The pre-fit, and why it is the precondition for everything}

The \textbf{pre-period fit} measures how closely the synthetic tracks the treated market
\emph{before} the campaign. It is summarised by the root-mean-square of the pre-launch gaps,
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{RMSE} \;=\; \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T_0}\sum_{t < T_0}\big(Y_{0t} - \widehat{Y}_{0,t}(0)\big)^{2}}\,,
  \label{eq:sc:rmse}
\end{equation}
%
the average size of a weekly miss over the pre-period (sometimes called the RMSPE). It is the only
quality check that exists, because it is the only stretch of time in which the truth is known: the
campaign had not run, so the gap \emph{ought} to be zero. A synthetic that cannot reproduce the
treated market's past has no business being trusted with its counterfactual future, and every
number downstream inherits that failure. This is \cref{as:sc:prefit}, and \cref{sec:sc:model}
enforces it as a pass/fail gate rather than reporting it as a statistic.

\subsection*{Placebo-in-space, and what a permutation \texorpdfstring{$p$}{p}-value is}

With one treated unit there is no standard error to read off a fit: a standard error describes
variability across many units, and here there is one. So inference is done by
\textbf{permutation}, and the idea is simpler than the name.

Pretend, in turn, that each \emph{untreated} donor market was the treated one. Refit the whole
method on it. Any ``lift'' that comes out is manufactured, because that market ran no campaign:
it is pure noise from the estimator itself. Do that for every donor and you have a distribution of
lifts under the null hypothesis that the campaign did nothing. That is the
\textbf{placebo-in-space} distribution, and \cref{fig:sc:placebodist} is it.

Now ask where the \emph{real} treated market falls in that distribution. If its lift is
unremarkable, comfortably inside the placebo spread, the campaign has not distinguished
itself from noise. If it sits out in the tail, it has. The \textbf{permutation $p$-value} is simply the
share of placebo markets whose (absolute) lift is at least as large as the treated market's. It
requires no likelihood, no normality, and no asymptotics; it asks only whether the treated market
is unusual \emph{among markets the method was run on identically}.
%
\begin{equation}
  p \;=\; \frac{\#\big\{\, j \in \text{donors} \;:\; |\hat\tau_j| \ \ge\ |\hat\tau_0| \,\big\} + 1}
               {J + 1},
  \label{eq:sc:vocab_perm}
\end{equation}
%
with the $+1$s the standard finite-sample correction: the treated market is itself one of the
$J + 1$ ways the label could have fallen, so it belongs in its own reference distribution.

\input{build/floats/nb07_placebo_dist}

\subsection*{The estimand: one week, or all twenty?}

Two quantities can be reported, and the chapter's central finding is about the difference between
them. The \textbf{per-week effect} is $\tau_t$ of \cref{eq:sc:vocab_gap}. The \textbf{total} is
their sum over the post-period,
%
\begin{equation}
  \tau \;=\; \sum_{t \ge T_0} \tau_t ,
  \label{eq:sc:vocab_total}
\end{equation}
%
and it is the total that the business buys: a campaign is paid for once, and its return is the
whole of what it earned. Keep the distinction in view, because a model can price the per-week
uncertainty well and the total's uncertainty poorly. \Cref{sec:sc:compare} is where the two are
separated and the total is priced honestly.

\begin{recap}
The data is a panel; one market was treated, the rest are the donor pool. The counterfactual is a
weighted average of donors (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_synth}), with weights fitted on the pre-period and
confined to the simplex (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_simplex}) so that the synthetic interpolates between
real markets and never extrapolates beyond them. The gap between the market and its synthetic
double is the estimated lift (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_gap}); the pre-period fit is the precondition for
believing it; the placebo-in-space distribution supplies inference without a likelihood; and the
quantity the decision actually consumes is the \emph{total} over the post-period
(\cref{eq:sc:vocab_total}), not the per-week path.
\end{recap}

% =====================================================================================
\section{The data-generating model}
\label{sec:sc:dgm}

A causal method cannot be graded on the data a business actually has, because there the
counterfactual is missing. That is the entire problem. It is graded, instead, in the three
tiers of \reffound{}: against a \emph{simulated} world whose answer is known by
construction (this section); against a \emph{within-study benchmark} whose answer is known by
randomization (\cref{sec:sc:real}, on a real geo experiment with a randomized control arm); and,
in deployment, not at all: only falsified, by tests it could have failed and did not.

The simulator produces a weekly sales panel: \nbSevenNDmas{} markets, indexed
$i = 0, \dots, \nbSevenNDmas-1$, over \nbSevenNWeeks{} weeks $t = 0, \dots, \nbSevenNWeeks-1$.
Three latent factors are \emph{shared by every market} (a secular trend $g_t$, a seasonal
cycle $s_t$, and a macroeconomic random walk $m_t$):
%
\begin{align}
  g_t &= \beta_g\,t, \label{eq:sc:trend}\\
  s_t &= a_1\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{2\pi t}{P_1}\Big) + a_2\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{2\pi t}{P_2}\Big),
        \qquad P_1 = 26 \text{ weeks},\ \ P_2 = 13 \text{ weeks},
        \label{eq:sc:season}\\
  m_t &= \sum_{u \le t} \eta_u, \qquad \eta_u \sim \mathcal N\!\left(0,\ \sigma_m^{2}\right).
        \label{eq:sc:macro}
\end{align}
%
Each market carries its own baseline $c_i \sim U(c_{\min}, c_{\max})$ and its own sensitivity to
each factor, its \emph{loadings} $\lambda_i = (\lambda_{i1}, \lambda_{i2}, \lambda_{i3})$, each
drawn $U(\lambda_{\min}, \lambda_{\max})$, and observed sales, in thousands of euros, are
%
\begin{equation}
  Y_{it} \;=\; c_i \;+\; \lambda_{i1}\,g_t \;+\; \lambda_{i2}\,s_t \;+\; \lambda_{i3}\,m_t
           \;+\; \varepsilon_{it},
  \qquad \varepsilon_{it} \sim \mathcal N\!\left(0,\ \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}\right).
  \label{eq:sc:dgp}
\end{equation}
%
The campaign is planted in market $0$ alone, from week \nbSevenLaunchWeek{} onwards, as a
proportional lift of $\kappa = \pc{\nbSevenLiftPct}$ on that market's untreated level:
%
\begin{equation}
  \Delta_t \;=\; \kappa \,\big(c_0 + \lambda_{01}\,g_t + \lambda_{02}\,s_t + \lambda_{03}\,m_t\big)
  \cdot \mathbf 1[\,t \ge \nbSevenLaunchWeek\,],
  \qquad Y_{0t} \;\mathrel{+}=\; \Delta_t .
  \label{eq:sc:lift}
\end{equation}
%
\Cref{tab:sc:params} gives every one of those constants, its value, and (the column that
matters) why it has that value rather than another. Two of them are load-bearing and deserve
naming here. $\sigma_m$, the scale of the macro random walk, is what makes the markets co-move at
all: set it to zero and the panel becomes thirty independent series, a synthetic control becomes
unnecessary, and the chapter has nothing to teach. And $\kappa$ is what the estimator must
recover; it is the truth, not a knob.

\input{build/tables/nb07_params}
%
Summed over the \nbSevenNPost{} post-launch weeks, the planted total lift is
\keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal}, an average of \keur{\nbSevenTrueAvg} per week. That number is the
grade every estimator in this chapter is marked against, and it is the last time in the chapter
that anything is known for certain.

\Cref{fig:sc:panel} shows the panel. The macro random walk $m_t$ of \cref{eq:sc:macro} is both
the villain and the hero of the story. It is the villain because it moves every market together,
so any before-and-after comparison will book its drift as campaign effect. It is the hero because
it is \emph{shared}: since all markets load on the same $(g_t, s_t, m_t)$, a convex combination
of donor markets whose blended loadings match $(\lambda_{01}, \lambda_{02}, \lambda_{03})$
reproduces market $0$'s untreated path exactly, up to idiosyncratic noise. That is what makes the
effect identifiable at all. It is also the reason the summed lift carries more uncertainty than an
independent-errors model admits: $m_t$ is a random walk, so whatever share of it the weights fail
to match persists across the whole post-period instead of averaging away. \Cref{sec:sc:compare}
prices that persistence and builds the interval that reflects it.

\input{build/floats/nb07_panel}

% =====================================================================================
\section{Identification}
\label{sec:sc:ident}

\subsection*{The estimand}

In the potential-outcome notation of \citet{rubin1974} and \citet{imbens2015}, write
$Y_{0,t}(1)$ for market $0$'s week-$t$ sales when the campaign runs and $Y_{0,t}(0)$ for its
sales when it does not. Only the first is observed. The causal effect in week $t$ is their
contrast, and the decision's estimand is the post-launch sum introduced in \cref{sec:sc:vocab}:
%
\begin{equation}
  \tau_t \;=\; Y_{0,t}(1) - Y_{0,t}(0), \qquad t \ge T_0,
  \qquad\qquad
  \tau \;=\; \sum_{t \ge T_0} \tau_t ,
  \label{eq:sc:estimand}
\end{equation}
%
with $T_0 = \nbSevenLaunchWeek$ the launch week. Everything in this chapter (the classical
estimator, the posterior, both intervals, and the euro decision) targets the single scalar
$\tau$ of \cref{eq:sc:estimand}. Keeping the estimand fixed is what makes the comparison in
\cref{sec:sc:compare} a comparison rather than a change of subject.

\subsection*{The estimator}

Synthetic control fills the hole in \cref{eq:sc:estimand} with the object \cref{sec:sc:vocab} already
built: the weighted average of donors $\widehat{Y}_{0,t}(0)$ of \cref{eq:sc:vocab_synth}, on the unit
simplex \cref{eq:sc:vocab_simplex}, and the gap \cref{eq:sc:vocab_gap} as the estimated lift. No new
notation is needed here; what identification adds is the set of conditions under which that estimator
recovers \cref{eq:sc:estimand}. \Cref{fig:sc:simplex} has already priced the simplex constraint: it
keeps the synthetic a blend of real markets rather than an extrapolation, which is what makes it
defensible in the post-period nobody can check \citep{abadie2010, abadie2021}.

\subsection*{What the estimator does to the confounding, on a graph}

It is worth stating, in the language of causal graphs (\reffound{}), exactly what this
estimator does to the problem it was built for. The disease is a \emph{backdoor}: latent factors
$(g_t, s_t, m_t)$, the season, the trend, and the macro economy, drive both the decision to
advertise in a market like this one \emph{and} that market's sales, so raw co-movement between
campaigns and sales mixes the causal arrow with the confounding fork
(\cref{fig:sc:dags}, left). Those confounders are unobserved, so they cannot be adjusted for
directly. What makes them tractable is that they are \emph{shared} across markets and stable over
time. Synthetic control therefore closes the backdoor by \emph{matching on the confounder's
footprint}: a donor blend whose loadings match the treated market's carries the same latent
$(g_t, s_t, m_t)$, and the gap $Y_{0t} - \widehat{Y}_{0,t}(0)$ cancels them
(\cref{fig:sc:dags}, centre). That is why the two assumptions that follow do all the work: the
convex hull (\cref{as:sc:hull}) says a twin with the same footprint \emph{exists}, and the pre-fit
(\cref{as:sc:prefit}) is the \emph{evidence} that the fitted twin really shares it. The other
observational cure, the instrumental variable (\refiv{}), leaves the backdoor standing and
\emph{bypasses} it, using only the slice of exposure that an exogenous instrument moves
(\cref{fig:sc:dags}, right). Same disease, two cures: synthetic control removes the backdoor; IV
routes around it.

\begin{figure}[tb]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=8mm and 7mm, baseline=(Xa.base)]
  % Panel (a): the disease
  \node[daglat]  (Ca) {$C$};
  \node[dagnode] (Xa) [below left=of Ca]  {spend};
  \node[dagnode] (Ya) [below right=of Ca] {sales};
  \draw[dagconf]  (Ca) -- (Xa);
  \draw[dagconf]  (Ca) -- (Ya);
  \draw[dagcause] (Xa) -- (Ya);
  \node[daglabel] at ($(Xa)!0.5!(Ya) + (0,-6mm)$) {(a) the backdoor};
\end{tikzpicture}
\hfill
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=8mm and 5mm, baseline=(Yb.base)]
  % Panel (b): synthetic control
  \node[daglat]  (Cb) {$C$};
  \node[dagnode] (Yb) [below left=of Cb]  {$Y_{0t}$};
  \node[dagnode] (Sb) [below right=of Cb] {$\widehat{Y}_{0,t}(0)$};
  \node[dagnode] (Tb) [left=5mm of Yb] {campaign};
  \draw[dagconf]  (Cb) -- (Yb);
  \draw[dagconf]  (Cb) -- (Sb);
  \draw[dagcause] (Tb) -- (Yb);
  \node[daglabel] at ($(Yb)!0.5!(Sb) + (0,-6mm)$) {(b) SC: the gap cancels $C$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\hfill
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=8mm and 7mm, baseline=(Xc.base)]
  % Panel (c): the instrument
  \node[daglat]  (Cc) {$C$};
  \node[dagnode] (Xc) [below left=of Cc]  {spend};
  \node[dagnode] (Yc) [below right=of Cc] {sales};
  \node[dagnode, draw=DagGreen] (Zc) [left=5mm of Xc] {$Z$};
  \draw[dagconf]  (Cc) -- (Xc);
  \draw[dagconf]  (Cc) -- (Yc);
  \draw[dagcause] (Xc) -- (Yc);
  \draw[daginst]  (Zc) -- (Xc);
  \node[daglabel] at ($(Xc)!0.5!(Yc) + (0,-6mm)$) {(c) IV: bypass via $Z \perp C$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{One disease, two cures. \emph{(a)} The backdoor: unobserved shared factors $C$ drive both
spend and sales, so co-movement overstates the causal arrow. \emph{(b)} Synthetic control removes
the backdoor: a donor blend with the treated market's loadings carries the same $C$, and the gap
cancels it; the pre-fit is the evidence the twin really shares $C$
(\cref{as:sc:hull,as:sc:prefit}). \emph{(c)} Instrumental variables (\refiv{}) bypasses it:
only the $Z$-driven slice of spend is used, and $Z$ owes nothing to $C$. Dashed nodes and edges
are unobserved and confounding; solid dark edges are causal; the green edge is the instrument.
This figure is a schematic, not a computed result.}
\label{fig:sc:dags}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{The assumptions}

Identification of \cref{eq:sc:estimand} by the estimator \cref{eq:sc:vocab_synth} rests on five
assumptions. They are stated here, and each is either tested or explicitly declared untestable
later in the chapter: an assumption with no accompanying test is a wish.

\begin{assumption}[Convex hull]\label{as:sc:hull}
The treated market lies inside the convex hull of the donor pool: there exists $w \in \simplex$
whose blend matches market $0$ on the \emph{full} feature vector that generates its path: its
baseline level and its three factor loadings alike,
$\sum_j w_j\,(c_j,\, \lambda_{j1},\, \lambda_{j2},\, \lambda_{j3})
   = (c_0,\, \lambda_{01},\, \lambda_{02},\, \lambda_{03})$. Both parts matter. The baseline
$c_i$ of \cref{eq:sc:dgp} is an additive, market-specific level. A blend that matches the
loadings but not the baseline leaves a constant gap; one that matches the baseline but not the
loadings leaves a drifting one. The simplex constraint enforces the hull by refusing to
extrapolate; the diagnostic is a pre-period fit that the weights cannot achieve.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Good pre-fit]\label{as:sc:prefit}
The synthetic tracks the treated market \emph{before} launch. Formally, the pre-period RMSE of
the gap \cref{eq:sc:vocab_gap} is small relative to the effect the design must detect. This is the
precondition on which every subsequent number depends: a synthetic that cannot reproduce the
treated market's past cannot be trusted with its counterfactual future. It is tested as a
pass/fail gate in \cref{sec:sc:model}.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[No anticipation]\label{as:sc:anticip}
Nothing shifts sales in the treated market \emph{before} $T_0$: no leaked creative, no
pre-buying, no mis-dated launch. Tested by placebo-in-time in \cref{sec:sc:wrong}.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Stable factor structure]\label{as:sc:stable}
The co-movement that held in the pre-period continues to hold after it: the loadings the weights
matched on weeks $0$ to $T_0 - 1$ still govern weeks $T_0$ onward. A long pre-period makes the
match harder to achieve by accident. Placebo-in-time probes it, but never verifies it: the
post-period loadings are unobservable by construction, so the probe runs at a placebo date, not at
the real one.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[No interference]\label{as:sc:sutva}
The campaign in market $0$ does not change sales in any donor market. This is the no-interference
half of \textbf{SUTVA}, the stable-unit-treatment-value assumption \citep{imbens2015}: one unit's
treatment does not spill onto another unit's outcome, and there is a single version of the treatment.
Applied to geographies, it is the assumption that no placebo can test,
because spillover contaminates every placebo refit in exactly the same way. It is stress-tested
by simulation in \cref{sec:sc:wrong}, where the news is better than it sounds: spillover
\emph{attenuates}.
\end{assumption}

\begin{remark}
A sixth condition, that nothing else hit market $0$ at exactly $T_0$, is genuinely
untestable from the panel and can be discharged only by institutional knowledge. Say so, rather
than dress a diagnostic in its clothes. \Cref{sec:sc:real} is instructive here: on a randomized
geo experiment, that assumption is guaranteed in expectation by the coin flip, and the difference
between the two situations is exactly the value of an experiment.
\end{remark}

% =====================================================================================
\section{The classical baseline}
\label{sec:sc:classical}

Synthetic control was born frequentist. \citeauthor{abadie2003}'s original estimator uses no
sampler, and the Bayesian model of \cref{sec:sc:bayes} re-houses it rather than replacing it. This
section fits that classical estimator in full, because on its own it carries the chapter's decision.

\subsection*{Fitting the weights by constrained least squares}

Choose the weights that best reproduce the treated market's pre-launch path, subject to
\cref{as:sc:hull}'s simplex:
%
\begin{equation}
  \hat w \;=\; \argmin_{w \,\in\, \simplex}\ \sum_{t < T_0}
    \Big( Y_{0t} - \sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\, Y_{jt} \Big)^{2}.
  \label{eq:sc:slsqp}
\end{equation}
%
This is constrained least squares (a quadratic objective on a convex set), solved numerically by
\textbf{sequential least-squares quadratic programming} (SLSQP), the routine the figures name
(\hyperref[app:sc:slsqp]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.1} sketches how it works). It is
deterministic, it runs in milliseconds, and it estimates nothing else: no variance parameter, no
prior, no chain. The
counterfactual and the effect follow by substitution into \cref{eq:sc:vocab_gap}. Because it is so
cheap, this same fitter carries every robustness check in the chapter: the leave-one-out
refits, the placebo distributions, the launch-date sweep, the spillover stress test.

Fitted to the pre-launch weeks, it puts material weight on \nbSevenClNMaterial{} of the
\nbSevenNDonors{} donors (an effective donor count of \nbSevenClEffDonors{}, measured by the
inverse Herfindahl index $1/\sum_j w_j^2$) and tracks the treated market to a pre-launch RMSE
of \keur{\nbSevenClPreRmse} (\cref{fig:sc:sc}). That is the tightest pre-fit any arm of this
chapter achieves. It clears the gate of \cref{as:sc:prefit} (a bar of
\keur{\nbSevenGateRef}, a third of the weekly lift the design must detect) by a margin no other
fit here enjoys. \Cref{sec:sc:model} grades the Bayesian fits on the same bar, and they do not
clear it. The post-launch gap comes to \keur{\nbSevenClAvg} per week and \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}
over the window, against a planted truth of \keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal}, a relative error of
$\nbSevenClErrPct\,\%$.

\input{build/floats/nb07_sc}

\subsection*{The placebo distribution}

There is no likelihood here, so there is no standard error to read off the fit, and a regression
standard error would answer the wrong question: it describes variability across many treated units,
and here there is one. Inference comes instead from the placebo-in-space permutation introduced in
\cref{sec:sc:vocab} \citep{abadie2010, abadie2015, fisher1935}, now run in earnest. Take each of the
\nbSevenNDonors{} donors in turn, pretend it was the treated market, and rebuild its synthetic from
the \emph{other} donors, holding the genuinely treated market out of every placebo pool because its
post-period is contaminated by the real campaign. The gap that comes out is a manufactured ``lift'':
the donor ran no campaign, so its post-period gap is pure estimator noise under the null that the
campaign did nothing. Those \nbSevenNDonors{} gaps are the reference distribution.

Two refinements of \citeauthor{abadie2010}'s sharpen the comparison, and they are separate ideas.
\emph{First, discard placebos with a poor pre-fit}: a donor whose own synthetic cannot track it
before launch produces a meaningless gap that would only pollute the reference distribution. The
conventional cutoff is a pre-period RMSE more than five times the treated unit's. Here it changes
nothing, and the reason is worth stating precisely: \textbf{the filter never fires}. All
\nbSevenPlaceboKept{} of the \nbSevenNDonors{} donors clear it at $2\times$, $5\times$ and $20\times$
alike, so $p$ holds at \nbSevenPFilterTwo{} and \nbSevenPFilterTwenty{} across that range. The result
is robust \emph{here} because the filter is non-binding \emph{here}, not because a well-chosen cutoff
rescued it; on a panel with a genuinely unmatchable donor (\cref{sec:sc:real} has one) the cutoff
would bind, and would then have to be defended rather than reported. \emph{Second, rank on the RMSE
ratio, not the raw gap}: post-period RMSE over pre-period RMSE is scale-free, so it compares large and
small markets fairly, and it refuses to reward a placebo that fits badly everywhere, since such a unit
inflates its numerator \emph{and} its denominator.

\subsection*{The permutation \texorpdfstring{$p$}{p}-value, and its floor}

The $p$-value is the share of units, treated plus placebos, whose statistic is at least the treated
market's (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_perm}, with the finite-sample $+1$ that counts the treated unit inside its
own reference set). On the RMSE ratio the treated market ranks \nbSevenTreatedRank{} of
\nbSevenNUnits{}, a ratio of \nbSevenTreatedRatio{} against a placebo median of
\nbSevenPlaceboMedianRatio{}, for $p = \nbSevenPRatio$ (\cref{fig:sc:placebo}). The raw-gap
permutation agrees exactly ($p = \nbSevenPGap$): the treated market is the most extreme unit in the
panel on both statistics.

That $p$ is not merely small; it is the \emph{smallest this design can produce}. With
$J = \nbSevenNDonors$ donors the $+1$ correction floors the $p$-value at
$1/(J+1) = 1/\nbSevenNUnits = \nbSevenPRatio$, and the treated market, ranked first, sits exactly on
it. No effect, however large, could push $p$ below \nbSevenPRatio{} on \nbSevenNUnits{} units. One
treated market buys significance but not resolution: the honest phrasing is ``as extreme as the design
can register,'' not a vanishing $p$-value.
(\hyperref[app:sc:perm]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.6} restates the test compactly.)

\input{build/floats/nb07_placebo}

\subsection*{The interval, by inverting the test}

The same null yields an \emph{interval}, and this is the step worth slowing down for. It rests on one
assumption, stated plainly: the effect is a \textbf{constant additive shift} over the post-period.
Under it, a candidate truth $\tau_0$ moves the \emph{location} of the estimate but not the
\emph{spread} of the estimator's error, so the placebo gaps, centred at zero, measure that spread
around \emph{any} $\tau_0$.

Inversion reads off which candidate truths the test does not reject. A value $\tau_0$ survives at the
90\,\% level exactly when the estimate lands in the middle 90\,\% of its error band around $\tau_0$,
and solving that for $\tau_0$ \emph{flips} the placebo percentiles:
%
\begin{equation*}
  \hat\tau \in \big[\,\tau_0 + q_{0.05},\ \tau_0 + q_{0.95}\,\big]
  \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
  \tau_0 \in \big[\,\hat\tau - q_{0.95},\ \hat\tau - q_{0.05}\,\big].
\end{equation*}
%
The reversal is the crux: the \emph{upper} placebo percentile sets the \emph{lower} bound, and the
lower percentile sets the upper. The placebo totals reach \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQhi} above zero (the
95th percentile) and \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQloMag} below it (the 5th), so with
$\hat\tau = \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}$ the upper percentile subtracts to the lower bound
(\keur{\nbSevenClTotal} $-$ \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQhi} $=$ \keur{\nbSevenRiLo}) while the lower, being
negative, \emph{adds} to the upper bound (\keur{\nbSevenClTotal} $+$ \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQloMag} $=$
\keur{\nbSevenRiHi}):
%
\begin{equation}
  \Big[\ \hat\tau - q_{0.95}\big(\tau^{\text{placebo}}\big),\ \
         \hat\tau - q_{0.05}\big(\tau^{\text{placebo}}\big)\ \Big]
  \;=\; \big[\,\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}\,\big].
  \label{eq:sc:ri}
\end{equation}
%
Call this the \textbf{randomization interval}: the set of candidate truths the placebo test does not
reject. \Cref{fig:sc:inversion} draws exactly how it is built: the placebo cloud at zero, the same cloud
inverted onto the estimate, with break-even inside the interval and zero far outside it. The interval is mildly
asymmetric, reaching \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQhi} below the estimate but \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboQloMag} above
it, so its midpoint sits a little above \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}; a symmetric $\pm$ summary would lose
that, and carrying the percentiles through keeps it. The planted \keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal} falls inside.
What earns this interval its trust is what it \emph{did not} do: it assumed no shape for the errors, no
normal, no $\sqrt{n}$, no likelihood. It measured the error distribution from markets known to be
untreated, autocorrelation and all, which is why \cref{sec:sc:compare} keeps it as the referee for the
posterior built there (\hyperref[app:sc:randint]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.8}).

\input{build/floats/nb07_inversion}

\subsection*{The margin trap}

Before pricing the campaign, one fact decides everything, and it is the thing decision-makers most
often get wrong: \textbf{advertising buys revenue, not profit}. The firm keeps only its
\pc{\nbSevenMargin} gross margin on the incremental sales. Write $c$ for the campaign's cost and $m$
for that margin. The campaign pays for itself only if the
lift clears a \textbf{break-even lift} of $b = c/m = \keur{\nbSevenBreakevenLift}$, equivalently only
if the incremental return on ad spend (\textbf{iROAS}, euros of incremental sales per euro spent)
beats a break-even iROAS of $1/m = \nbSevenBreakevenIroas$.

Here is the trap. The estimated return is an iROAS of \nbSevenIroas{}, and stated gross that
\emph{looks} like an obvious win: nearly three euros of sales for every euro spent. But once the margin
is applied the retained edge is almost nothing. The \keur{\nbSevenClTotal} of lift is worth only
\keur{\nbSevenClProfit} of profit, and against the \keur{\nbSevenCost} paid the net is
\keur{\nbSevenClNet} (\cref{fig:sc:marginfunnel}). A decision-maker who reasons in gross iROAS,
``2.9$\times$, ship it,'' walks straight into a break-even bet. \textbf{That \keur{\nbSevenClNet} of net
is not the size of the opportunity; it is the thin true edge hiding behind an impressive gross number.}
Everything the rest of this section does is measure how safe that thin edge is.

\input{build/floats/nb07_margin_funnel}

\subsection*{The decision at \keur{\nbSevenCost}}
\label{sec:sc:classicaldecision}

The classical arm now has everything a frequentist decision needs, on one scale: a point estimate, a
design-based interval that \cref{sec:sc:valid} confirms keeps its promise, and a significance test.
Read it in three steps.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
  \item \textbf{Is the lift real?} Yes. The placebo test ranks the treated market \nbSevenTreatedRank{}
        of \nbSevenNUnits{} ($p = \nbSevenPRatio$), and the randomization interval
        $[\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}]$ excludes zero.
  \item \textbf{Does the point estimate clear break-even?} Barely. The lift is \keur{\nbSevenClTotal},
        an iROAS of \nbSevenIroas{} against the \nbSevenBreakevenIroas{} bar, worth
        \keur{\nbSevenClProfit} of profit against the \keur{\nbSevenCost} paid: a net of just
        \keur{\nbSevenClNet}.
  \item \textbf{Is that margin safe?} No. Carry the whole interval through the margin and the profit
        runs from \keur{\nbSevenClProfitLo} to \keur{\nbSevenClProfitHi}, straddling the
        \keur{\nbSevenCost} cost. Equivalently the break-even lift \keur{\nbSevenBreakevenLift} sits
        well inside $[\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}]$, and the break-even iROAS
        \nbSevenBreakevenIroas{} sits inside the interval's iROAS range
        $[\nbSevenRiIroasLo,\ \nbSevenRiIroasHi]$. The lift is significantly \emph{positive}; it is not
        significantly \emph{profitable}.
\end{enumerate}

The verdict is \textbf{\nbSevenDecision}: a real effect this evidence cannot call a paying one, and a
frequentist does not roll out nationally on a lift whose interval includes losing money. No posterior
was consulted, and none was needed.

The one thing the classical arm cannot do is put a single number on \emph{how close} the call is: the
straddle in step~3 is a yes/no fact about an interval, not a probability. That single number,
$\Prob(\text{profit} > \text{cost})$, is the one addition the Bayesian layer makes (\cref{sec:sc:euro}),
and it does not overturn this verdict. First, though, the classical arm has more to say than a
single-price verdict, and the rest of this section draws it out.

\subsection*{Spanning the decision space: a cost sweep}

The \keur{\nbSevenCost} case is one point in a space, and the space teaches more than the point. Hold
the estimate and the interval fixed and slide the cost. Two thresholds organise everything, and the
lesson is that they are \emph{different}. The first is where the point estimate breaks even: the net
$m\hat\tau - c$ crosses zero at $c = m\hat\tau = \keur{\nbSevenClProfit}$. The second is where the
\emph{interval} stops straddling. Carried through the margin, the profit band is
$m\times[\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}]
= [\keur{\nbSevenClProfitLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenClProfitHi}]$. Any cost inside that band leaves the
call genuinely hard. Below \keur{\nbSevenClProfitLo} the whole band clears cost: a clean \textsc{go}.
Above \keur{\nbSevenClProfitHi} none of it does: a clean \textsc{no-go}. The two thresholds do not
coincide, and the gap between them, the straddle band around the mean's break-even at
\keur{\nbSevenClProfit}, is exactly the range of prices where a positive mean and an honest interval can
disagree (\cref{fig:sc:costsweep}).

\Cref{tab:sc:costsweep} works five costs that span that logic.

\input{build/tables/nb07_cost_sweep}
\input{build/floats/nb07_cost_sweep_plot}

The bookends bound the space. At \keur{\nbSevenSweepGoC} the break-even lift is \keur{\nbSevenSweepGoBe},
below the interval, so the whole profit band clears cost: a clean \textsc{go}, net \keur{\nbSevenSweepGoNet}
at the point estimate. At \keur{\nbSevenSweepNgC} the break-even lift is \keur{\nbSevenSweepNgBe}, above
the interval, net \keur{\nbSevenSweepNgNet}: a clean \textsc{no-go}. In both the straddle has ended,
which is precisely why the call is easy; they are ``clear'' not because the effect changed but because
break-even moved off the interval.

The two interior cases are the instructive ones, because in each the interval straddles and the
\emph{mean alone would mislead}. At \keur{\nbSevenSweepLoC} the net at the point estimate is a
healthy-looking \keur{\nbSevenSweepLoNet}, yet the break-even lift \keur{\nbSevenSweepLoBe} still sits
inside $[\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}]$: a comfortable mean the interval refuses to bless,
the sharpest reminder that the decision is not the mean. It is also \emph{our} case:
\keur{\nbSevenSweepLoC} is what the campaign actually cost. At \keur{\nbSevenSweepHiC} the sign has
flipped: the net is \keur{\nbSevenSweepHiNet}. But the break-even lift \keur{\nbSevenSweepHiBe} is
\emph{still} inside the interval, so the upper tail still reaches profitability. This \textsc{no-go}
leaves real money on the table, and it is the case where better evidence might be worth buying.
Pricing that question needs a probability the classical arm does not produce,
so it waits for the Bayesian layer, which puts a figure on it in \cref{sec:sc:euro}.% VOI hook now closed: the value-of-information number in sec:sc:euro is the answer to this (EUR 98k) case, where the hold still carries upside.

The knife-edge sits at \keur{\nbSevenSweepKeC}: net \keur{\nbSevenSweepKeNet}, break-even
\keur{\nbSevenSweepKeBe} near the centre of the interval, the hardest price in the space. The campaign
was not bought there. It was bought at \keur{\nbSevenCost}, in the first interior case, where the mean
is comfortable and the interval still withholds its blessing; pricing exactly that gap is what the
Bayesian layer is for.

One last honesty. The interval could be turned into a smooth probability by assuming the total is
normal with standard deviation $\sigma_\tau \approx \keur{\nbSevenSigmaTauApprox}$ (the 90\,\%
half-width over $1.645$) and reading $\Prob(\text{profit} > c)$ off it at any cost. That is an
illustration, not a classical output: the true total is right-skewed, so such normal figures sit a few
points off near break-even, and the classical arm deliberately stops at the interval. Turning it into
an honest single-number probability is the Bayesian arm's job (\cref{sec:sc:euro}).

\begin{remark}[Felt stakes]
The example is deliberately small, and a reader may object that no board agonises over
\keur{\nbSevenClNet}. Scale the whole campaign up by \nbSevenFeltN$\times$ and every ratio is
untouched, the iROAS, the margin, the straddle, the verdict, while the numbers become
\eur{\nbSevenFeltSpend}\,M of spend, \eur{\nbSevenFeltLift}\,M of lift, and \keur{\nbSevenFeltNet} of
net. This changes the \emph{felt} stakes, not the lesson: a reader who checks the iROAS sees the same
four-in-five bet, a solid expected net that still falls short of a confident buy. The point survives at any scale; the figures here are kept
small only so the arithmetic stays legible.
\end{remark}

% =====================================================================================
\section{Why add a posterior at all?}
\label{sec:sc:bayes}

The classical arm of \cref{sec:sc:classical} has already made the call, and made it without a
likelihood: a real effect the evidence cannot confidently price above cost, a profit interval that
straddles the price paid. That is a yes/no fact about where an interval sits, not a number a board
can weigh. A board reasons in probabilities, $\Prob(\text{profit} > c)$, and a permutation
$p$-value cannot supply one: it attaches a probability to a \emph{procedure} under a null, not to a
statement about $\tau$. \Cref{eq:sc:ri} says how often a test would reject, not how much posterior
mass sits above cost. The posterior is the only object that returns $\Prob(\text{profit} > c)$, and
that is what this arm adds: the same identification, the same estimand, the same number, now
carrying a distribution.

One contract governs everything that follows, and stating it up front is what lets the rest of the
chapter air its caveats freely. \textbf{The decision is already settled.} \Cref{sec:sc:classical}
reached it on the classical evidence alone, and nothing in the Bayesian sections moves the
\emph{action}; what the posterior supplies is the \emph{probability} that states that same decision
honestly. So a reader who wants only the rollout call has it and can skip to \cref{sec:sc:euro}; a
reader who wants the probability, and the one honest-uncertainty subtlety it drags in, reads on.
The verdict is banked. So the diagnostics ahead can be shown in full, including the place where the
posterior's first likelihood is simply wrong, without ever putting the call at risk.

% =====================================================================================
\section{The model: a Dirichlet prior and an iid likelihood}
\label{sec:sc:model}

The natural probability distribution over the simplex is the Dirichlet: its draws are
non-negative and sum to one by construction, so it says ``the weights live on $\simplex$'' in the
language of priors rather than constraints. Fitting the pre-period gives a posterior over the
weights, hence over the whole counterfactual path, hence over $\tau$: \cref{eq:sc:slsqp}'s
objective with uncertainty attached.

The model, as any analyst would first write it and fitted on the pre-period only ($t < T_0$), is
%
\begin{align}
  Y_{0t} \mid w, \sigma &\;\sim\; \mathcal N\Big(\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\, Y_{jt},\ \
                                   \sigma^{2}\Big), \qquad t < T_0, \label{eq:sc:lik}\\
  w &\;\sim\; \text{Dirichlet}(\mathbf 1_J), \label{eq:sc:prior_w}\\
  \sigma &\;\sim\; \text{HalfNormal}(5). \label{eq:sc:prior_s}
\end{align}
%
$\text{Dirichlet}(\mathbf 1_J)$ is flat over the simplex: no donor is favoured a priori. The
prior on $\sigma$ is weakly informative and tied to the units of the data. Sales are in
thousands of euros, the market baselines of \cref{eq:sc:dgp} sit between \keur{\nbSevenParLevelLo}
and \keur{\nbSevenParLevelHi} (\cref{tab:sc:params}), and the idiosyncratic noise has standard
deviation \keur{\nbSevenParNoiseSd}. So a scale of €5\,k is wide enough to accommodate
any plausible blend error and tight enough to exclude an absurd one.

The model is short enough to read in full. In PyMC \citep{salvatier2016} it is the three lines that
state \cref{eq:sc:lik}, \cref{eq:sc:prior_w} and \cref{eq:sc:prior_s}, and one call to the sampler:

\begin{pycode}
with pm.Model() as model:
    w     = pm.Dirichlet("w", a=np.ones(J))   # on the simplex
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", 5.0)       # blend-error scale
    mu    = pm.math.dot(w, Y_donors_pre)      # the synthetic control
    pm.Normal("y", mu=mu, sigma=sigma, observed=Y_treated_pre)
    idata = pm.sample()                       # NUTS
\end{pycode}

Everything downstream (the posterior over $\tau$, both intervals, the euro decision) is read off the
object this returns.

One word in \cref{eq:sc:lik} is doing more work than the two priors combined, and it is not
written down: \emph{independently}. The weekly errors are taken to be independent draws from
$\mathcal N(0, \sigma^2)$. That assumption is the default of every textbook regression, and
invisible on the page. Here it matters, because the quantity the rollout turns on is a total
summed over twenty correlated weeks. Independence understates how much such a total can vary.
\Cref{sec:sc:valid} measures the consequence for the interval, and \cref{sec:sc:compare} gives the
error the persistence it actually has and builds the interval the decision needs. The distinction
is carried in the notation from here on: the fit of \cref{eq:sc:lik} is the \emph{iid} fit, and
the model with the autocorrelated residual, built in \cref{sec:sc:compare}, is the \emph{shipped}
fit, the one whose posterior signs the euro decision. The flat Dirichlet prior does little work
here; it is the likelihood that shapes the posterior. \Cref{fig:sc:priorsens} makes that concrete:
refit under three Dirichlet concentrations, from sparse to diffuse, and the posterior of the total
lift barely moves. Two caveats keep the claim honest. First, the sweep is run on the iid likelihood
of \cref{eq:sc:lik}, the fit this chapter later discards; but the point is about the prior, not that
fit, and it carries to the repaired model unchanged, because forty pre-period weeks swamp the prior
either way. Second, prior stability covers the total's \emph{location and scale}, not everything the prior
touches. The concentration still moves the effective donor count: sparse blends lean on a few
donors, diffuse ones spread over many. That is a lever for out-of-sample stability and
interpretability. It does not shift the headline total.

\input{build/floats/nb07_prior_sensitivity}

The counterfactual for a post-launch week is the posterior \emph{predictive} blend,
%
\begin{equation}
  \widehat{Y}_{0t}(0) \;=\; \sum_j w_j\, Y_{jt} \;+\; \varepsilon_t,
  \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2),
  \label{eq:sc:ppc}
\end{equation}
%
and the $\varepsilon_t$ term earns its place. Dropping it (reporting the band of the mean
blend alone) would carry weight uncertainty but not the treated market's own noise, and so would
understate the per-week band. Posterior draws of $\tau$ follow by summing
$Y_{0t} - \widehat{Y}_{0t}(0)$ over the window.

Inference is by the No-U-Turn sampler \citep{hoffman2014} as implemented in PyMC
\citep{salvatier2016}: \nbSevenNDraws{} post-warmup draws, with $\hat R = \nbSevenRhat$, a
minimum bulk effective sample size of \nbSevenEss{} \citep{vehtari2021}, and \nbSevenDivergences{}
divergent transitions on the shipped fit. The chains on this panel have converged, so nothing
that follows on the simulated data is a sampler artefact. (\Cref{sec:sc:real} reports the
real-data fits' diagnostics separately.)

Both Bayesian fits put weight on many more donors than \cref{eq:sc:slsqp} did,
\nbSevenBayesEffDonors{} effective donors in the shipped posterior against the classical
\nbSevenClEffDonors{} (\cref{tab:sc:weights}), because a flat Dirichlet regularizes the blend.
That is a real trade and worth naming: the regularized blend is more stable out of sample, and
less interpretable as a business object. ``Our metro behaves like this specific mix of four named
metros'' is a sentence a media planner can act on; ``like a diffuse average of two dozen'' is not.

\input{build/tables/nb07_weights}

Fitted, \cref{eq:sc:lik} gives a posterior mean total lift of \keur{\nbSevenIidTotal}, with a
90\,\% credible interval of $[\keur{\nbSevenIidLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenIidHi}]$: width
\keur{\nbSevenIidWidth}, against the classical randomization interval's \keur{\nbSevenRiWidth}.
A likelihood-based interval barely three-fifths the width of a design-based one, on the same estimand
and the same data, is the sort of bargain that ought to invite scrutiny before it invites celebration.
And
the planted \keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal} sits \emph{outside} that band. One panel is one draw of the
world and a single miss proves nothing, so that observation is not yet a finding.
\Cref{sec:sc:valid} turns it into one.

\subsection*{Does the blend fit? The pre-fit gate}

\Cref{as:sc:prefit} is a gate, not a formality, and it is made concrete rather than eyeballed: the
pre-period RMSE must fall below a third of the weekly lift the design is meant to detect (the
\emph{estimated} weekly lift, since a diagnostic that consults the planted truth is not available on
real data). \textbf{The fit that carries the decision passes it.} Abadie's classical synthetic
control of \cref{sec:sc:classical} tracks the treated market to a pre-RMSE of \keur{\nbSevenClPreRmse}
against the \keur{\nbSevenGateRef} bar, comfortably inside. The decision of \cref{sec:sc:classical}
rests on a synthetic that clears its own precondition, so the example is sound.

The regularized Bayesian blend is a \emph{different} fit, and the looser of the two. Its flat
Dirichlet spreads weight over \nbSevenBayesEffDonors{} effective donors against the classical
\nbSevenClEffDonors{}, which stabilises the blend out of sample but tracks the pre-period less
tightly, at \keur{\nbSevenBayesPreRmse}, over the bar (\textbf{\nbSevenGate}). This is worth knowing,
not alarming: the two fits agree on the estimate to within \keur{\nbSevenEstDiff}
(\cref{tab:sc:compare}), so the looser blend inherits the credibility of the tighter one rather than
undermining it. Where the gate has real teeth is a donor pool that \emph{no} blend can match, and
\cref{sec:sc:real} exhibits one: the country's largest market lies outside its own donor hull, the
pre-fit fails loudly, and the method refuses to ship an estimate at all. That is the gate doing its
job, and it is the failure worth showing.

What holds for \emph{both} fits is that the pre-launch gap averages \keur{\nbSevenPreGapMean},
indistinguishable from zero (\cref{fig:sc:gap}): no \emph{systematic} mis-fit, just the shape a good
synthetic makes, hugging zero before launch and departing after. The design's limitation is precision,
not bias: the week-to-week wobble is of the same order as the weekly lift, so this design sits near
the edge of its own resolution. Read the euro call of \cref{sec:sc:euro} with that caution, and
prefer a longer pre-period where one exists. Do not distrust the number itself: the gate-passing
classical fit and the randomized experiment of \cref{sec:sc:real} both corroborate it.

\input{build/floats/nb07_gap}

\subsection*{Donor robustness}

If the estimate hangs on one market, it is not an estimate but an anecdote. Dropping each of the
top-weighted donors in turn and refitting moves the total only within
\keur{\nbSevenLooLo}--\keur{\nbSevenLooHi}, against the same fitter's all-donor baseline of
\keur{\nbSevenClTotal}. No single donor drives the result.

% =====================================================================================
\section{The interval is broken, and why}
\label{sec:sc:valid}

The fit passes its diagnostics: it tracks the pre-period and hangs on no single donor. That says
nothing about whether the \emph{interval} it reports is any good, and this section shows that the
first likelihood's is not. It fails on exactly the object the rollout buys, a total summed over
twenty weeks, and the failure has a single, diagnosable cause.

\subsection*{Calibration across fresh panels: the coverage failure}

This is the check that pays for the simulator, and the one that turns the chapter. A single panel
is a single draw of the world. The honest question about an interval is not whether it happens to
cover this time, but how often it covers. So the whole procedure (simulate, fit, interval) is
re-run on \nbSevenNPanels{} fresh panels drawn from \cref{eq:sc:dgp}, and the coverage of each
nominal 90\,\% interval is counted.

Three results, and they do not point the same way.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
  \item \textbf{Recovery is acceptable, precision is not.} The mean estimation error across panels
        is $\nbSevenSeedBias$ (EUR 000), or $\nbSevenSeedBiasPct\,\%$ of the truth, a modest
        bias. But the seed-to-seed standard deviation of that error is \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd}.
        Synthetic control here is a high-variance estimator, not a conservative one.
  \item \textbf{The per-week bands are honest.} The iid fit's 90\,\% posterior-predictive band
        covers the true weekly path \pc{\nbSevenWeekCov} of the time, essentially nominal. The
        observation-noise term of \cref{eq:sc:ppc} earns this.
  \item \textbf{The total-lift interval is not.} The same fit's 90\,\% credible interval on $\tau$
        covers the planted total on only \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} of panels (\cref{fig:sc:coverage}). A nominal ninety that
        delivers \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} is not a rounding error. It is a broken instrument, and
        the object it is broken about is precisely the one the business buys.
\end{enumerate}

That the same posterior can be honest about a week and dishonest about a sum of twenty of them is
the whole puzzle, and the answer is in the summation, not in the sampler.

\input{build/floats/nb07_coverage}

The classical arm, run on the very same panels, covers at \pc{\nbSevenRiCov}: it keeps its
promise. \Cref{tab:sc:compare} records both, and a third row whose likelihood is the business of
the next section. One honesty about the ruler itself: \nbSevenNPanels{} panels carry Monte-Carlo
error of their own, a binomial standard error of several points on every coverage rate
(\hyperref[app:sc:mcerr]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.17}). The robust reading is therefore not the
digits but the split: \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} is a broken instrument by any reckoning, while the
repaired and design-based rates of \cref{sec:sc:compare} are indistinguishable from the nominal
ninety at this resolution. Every panel here is also fitted with the repaired model of
\cref{sec:sc:compare}, so the comparison that follows is measured on identical worlds rather than
argued.

The coverage number just measured is the symptom; the rest of this section prices it and finds its
cause, and \cref{sec:sc:compare} builds the cure. Nothing here moves the decision, only the
probability that states it, so what follows makes the \emph{posterior} as honest as the design-based
interval \cref{sec:sc:euro} will lean on. One quantity is at fault: the \emph{width}
of the interval on the \nbSevenNPost-week total. We first dispose of the point estimate, where
classical and Bayesian simply agree, then spend the rest of the section on that width in two steps:
the posterior's interval is too narrow, and the cause is a persistent offset in the week-to-week
error. The cure, one extra parameter, is the business of \cref{sec:sc:compare}.

\input{build/tables/nb07_compare}

\subsection*{The lift, and why classical and Bayesian agree on it}

\Cref{tab:sc:compare} puts all three arms on the estimand of \cref{eq:sc:estimand}. The classical
point estimate and the shipped posterior differ by \keur{\nbSevenEstDiff}
(\pc{\nbSevenEstDiffPct} of the effect) and every arm, the discarded iid fit included, sits
\emph{below} the planted truth. Neither paradigm is the closer of the two in any sense worth a
sentence: the difference between them is a fraction of a standard deviation. The shared undershoot
is a property of \emph{this draw}, not of this design. The chapter has the evidence to say
which: the calibration check above refit everything on \nbSevenNPanels{} fresh panels and found a
mean error of \keur{\nbSevenSeedBias}, an \emph{over}-shoot. One panel cannot tell you about bias.
That is what the fresh-panel check is for, and it is why a single-panel undershoot must not be
promoted into a property of the method. The
Bayesian layer did not buy a better point estimate, and it could not have: the causal work was
done by the identification of \cref{sec:sc:ident} and by the simplex, and all three arms share it.
This is the ordinary case, and it has a name, the Bernstein--von Mises phenomenon
\citep{gelman2013} (\hyperref[app:sc:bvm]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.13}): with a well-specified likelihood and enough data, the posterior concentrates
where the maximum-likelihood estimate does, and the two paradigms return the same number.

One qualification keeps that story honest. The agreement is between the classical fit and the
\emph{shipped} posterior, the one \cref{sec:sc:compare} builds with a first-order autoregressive
(AR(1)) error; the discarded iid posterior is the instructive exception. At
\keur{\nbSevenIidTotal} it sits well below the classical \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}, and that gap is the
likelihood at work on the \emph{estimate}, not only on the interval. An AR(1) likelihood is a
generalised-least-squares re-weighting of the pre-period (\hyperref[app:sc:ar1]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.10}),
so getting the error model wrong changes which weeks the fit trusts, hence the weights, hence the
centre. Identification and the simplex put every arm in the right neighbourhood; the likelihood still
nudges the centre \emph{within} it, and the misspecified iid fit nudged it the wrong way. So ``the
sampler adds no causal content'' is right, but ``the likelihood touches only the width'' is not: the
repair of \cref{sec:sc:compare} moves the estimate too, from \keur{\nbSevenIidTotal} up to
\keur{\nbSevenBayesTotal}, which lands \emph{beside} the classical \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}.

It is worth being exact about what that agreement is, because it is easy to overclaim. The AR(1)
repair does \emph{not} recover the classical \emph{weights}. Abadie's constrained least squares
concentrates the blend on \nbSevenClEffDonors{} effective donors; the flat Dirichlet spreads the
same reconstruction over \nbSevenBayesEffDonors{} (\cref{fig:sc:weightprofiles,tab:sc:weights}), and
re-weighting the pre-period never pulls the second profile onto the first. What the two arms share
is the \emph{total}, reached by two different routes. That is corroboration across weightings, and
it is worth more than one method reproducing the other: two estimators that disagree on which markets
to trust, yet agree on the answer, are harder to dismiss than either alone. The regularized blend
remains the more stable of the two out of sample and the less interpretable, the trade
\cref{sec:sc:model} already named; it is made on the weights, not on the estimate.

\input{build/floats/nb07_weightprofiles}

\subsection*{The iid interval is too narrow}

With the centre settled, the width. Start with the symptom, because it is stark: the calibration
check above found that the iid posterior's nominal 90\,\% interval on the \nbSevenNPost-week total
covers the truth on only \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} of fresh panels, against \pc{\nbSevenWeekCov} for its
\emph{per-week} band. \Cref{fig:sc:iidshipped} shows the gap in one picture: the iid posterior (the
narrow one) sits well inside both the design-based interval and the wider autocorrelated posterior
\cref{sec:sc:compare} builds, and on this panel the planted truth falls \emph{outside} the iid band. The
failure is confined to the \emph{sum}; the per-week band is fine. So the rest of the section prices a
single number, the standard deviation of the total, and asks why the iid likelihood gets it too small.

\input{build/floats/nb07_iid_vs_shipped}

\subsection*{How wide should the interval on the total be?}

The decision needs to know how far the \nbSevenNPost-week total could plausibly fall from the
estimate, so the object to price is the standard deviation of that total. Four quantities claim to
price it, and \cref{tab:sc:scales} sets them side by side. The iid posterior of \cref{eq:sc:lik}
says \keur{\nbSevenIidSd}. The autocorrelated posterior built below says \keur{\nbSevenBayesSd}. The
placebo distribution says \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboSd}. And the yardstick (the \emph{actual} spread
of the estimation error across the \nbSevenNPanels{} fresh panels, which only a simulator can
supply) says \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd}.

\input{build/tables/nb07_scales}

The iid posterior is the outlier. It underprices the fresh-panel spread by a factor of
\nbSevenUnderprice{}, and the coverage column of \cref{tab:sc:compare} is the direct consequence: a
standard deviation two-and-a-bit times too small produces a band that is right half the time. That
is the width the decision cannot use, and the next subsection explains what in the errors produces
it.

Grading the two posteriors fairly takes one piece of care, which
\hyperref[app:sc:referee]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.9} works through: each must be judged against the
spread of \emph{its own} estimator across fresh panels, because the iid and autocorrelated fits make
different errors. Judged that way the verdict is clean. The iid posterior is genuinely too narrow.
The autocorrelated posterior, whose own fresh-panel spread is \keur{\nbSevenSeedSdArone}, reports
within ten per cent of that \emph{on average across panels} (\keur{\nbSevenAroneCrosspanelSd}) and is
calibrated rather than tight. The right comparison is that cross-panel average
\keur{\nbSevenAroneCrosspanelSd} against the \keur{\nbSevenSeedSdArone} spread. The
\keur{\nbSevenBayesSd} in \cref{tab:sc:scales} is only what this one panel's posterior reports, and
a single panel scatters around the average.

The design-based placebo scale earns a sentence of its own, because it separates an efficiency loss
from a validity one. At \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboSd} it sits \pc{\nbSevenPlaceboSdErrPct} \emph{below}
the yardstick (it too under-prices) and yet the randomization interval still covers at
\pc{\nbSevenRiCov} against a nominal ninety. \Cref{eq:sc:ri} does not assume a normal with that
standard deviation. It inverts the placebo \emph{quantiles}, so what it needs is a reference
distribution of roughly the right \emph{shape}, not one of exactly the right scale. It gets one,
and it covers. The iid posterior gets neither: it commits to an error structure that is false, so
its interval is both too narrow and too rarely right: \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov}. Being approximately
right about the spread costs efficiency; being wrong about the error structure costs validity.

\subsection*{Why an independent-errors interval is too narrow}

The cause is a single modelling choice. The likelihood \cref{eq:sc:lik} treats the weekly errors as
independent, and the uncertainty of an independent total grows only as $\sqrt{n}$. But the blend's
error is not independent from week to week. Whatever share of the treated market's factor loadings
the weights fail to match leaks the \emph{shared} factors straight into the gap. And one of those
factors, the macro random walk $m_t$ of \cref{eq:sc:macro}, barely turns around over the
post-period. So the mismatch acts as a near-constant \emph{offset} (one draw of ``how well did
this donor pool match?'', held for the whole post-period) sitting on top of ordinary weekly
noise.

Two things follow from an error of that shape, and \hyperref[app:sc:acf]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.11}
derives both. First, its autocorrelation is \emph{flat}: it does not decay with the lag, because a
persistent offset is equally present at every horizon. Second, the variance of the summed total
picks up a term in $n^2$ rather than $n$. So the total is far more uncertain than $\sqrt{n}$ scaling
implies, while the \emph{per-week} band, which needs only the one-week variance, is left alone.
That is exactly the split the coverage numbers show: the iid per-week band covers at
\pc{\nbSevenWeekCov}, and only its total-lift band fails.

\Cref{fig:sc:acf} measures the autocorrelation rather than assuming it, on placebo worlds where the
true lift is zero and the post-launch gap therefore \emph{is} the error. The plotted quantity is an
\emph{uncentered cross-panel} statistic: the ratio of between-panel offset variance to total gap
variance at each lag, not the within-series autocorrelation of any one panel. That is the
right object here, because it is precisely the persistent offset it exposes. It reads
$\rho_1 = \nbSevenRhoOne$ and, decisively, still $\nbSevenRhoEight$ at lag eight: flat, not
decaying. The error is not \emph{strongly} correlated; it is \emph{persistently} correlated, and
the two call for different cures.

\input{build/floats/nb07_acf}

Translating that structure into a standard deviation for the \nbSevenNPost-week total gives
\keur{\nbSevenTotalSdTrue} against the \keur{\nbSevenTotalSdIid} an iid likelihood charges: an
under-pricing of \nbSevenTotalSdRatio$\times$ from the error's structure alone, and
\nbSevenUnderprice$\times$ measured directly across fresh panels (\keur{\nbSevenSeedSd} against
\keur{\nbSevenIidSd}). The two agree on the factor, which is the claim.
\hyperref[app:sc:acf]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.11} carries the offset-model derivation, the
$\sigma_\omega/\sigma_\varepsilon$ decomposition that reproduces the measured autocorrelation, and the
in-sample residual check that shows why the pre-period fit could never have warned of any of this.

% =====================================================================================
\section{The fix: an AR(1) residual}
\label{sec:sc:compare}

The diagnosis is complete: the errors carry a persistent offset the iid likelihood pinned to zero,
so the interval on the total is too narrow. The fix is to give the likelihood that persistence, and
one extra parameter does it.

\subsection*{The interval that gets the total right}

The remedy is to give the model the error structure the diagnostic just found, rather than the
independence it assumed, the route of the Bayesian structural time-series literature
\citep{brodersen2015}. Let the residual $e_t = Y_{0t} - \sum_j w_j Y_{jt}$ follow an AR(1) law,
%
\begin{equation}
  e_t \;=\; \rho\, e_{t-1} \;+\; \varepsilon_t,
        \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^{2}), \label{eq:sc:ar1}
\end{equation}
%
substitute it into the likelihood so the model is written entirely in observables, and keep the
same flat Dirichlet on $w$: exactly one new parameter.
\hyperref[app:sc:ar1]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.10} gives that observable form, including the stationary
initial condition, and shows why an AR(1) likelihood is a generalised-least-squares re-weighting of
the pre-period. The half that is routinely forgotten is the predictive. The post-launch residual
must be \emph{simulated forward} under \cref{eq:sc:ar1}, not redrawn independently each week,
because it is the persistence \emph{across the post window} that makes the total's variance
compound.

An alternative repair leaves the fit untouched and rescales the posterior after the fact:
\citet{mueller2013} shows that a sandwich covariance can restore an interval's frequentist coverage
under a misspecified likelihood. Either route fixes the width; this chapter takes the AR(1) because
it also hands the analyst a parameter to read.

The data carried this parameter's value all along. The posterior on $\rho$ is centred at
\nbSevenRhoPost{} with a 90\,\% interval of $[\nbSevenRhoLo,\ \nbSevenRhoHi]$, the quantity the
iid likelihood had silently pinned to zero. The fix does three things.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
  \item \textbf{It moves the estimate, and it lands beside the classical number.} The AR(1)
        posterior mean is \keur{\nbSevenBayesTotal} against the iid fit's \keur{\nbSevenIidTotal}, a
        shift of more than one iid posterior standard deviation. An AR(1) likelihood re-weights
        which pre-period weeks the fit trusts, hence the donor weights, hence the counterfactual,
        hence the estimate (\hyperref[app:sc:ar1]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.10}), so the width was never
        going to move alone. Where it lands is the corroboration: \emph{beside} the classical arm's
        \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}, not on top of it, and reached by a different weight profile
        (\cref{fig:sc:weightprofiles}). Identification is untouched; the estimator is not, so the
        repair buys a corroborated centre and an honest width at once.
  \item \textbf{It re-prices the interval.} The 90\,\% credible interval becomes
        $[\keur{\nbSevenBayesLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenBayesHi}]$, a width of \keur{\nbSevenBayesWidth}
        against the iid band's \keur{\nbSevenIidWidth}, wider by a factor of
        \nbSevenWidthRatio{}. It contains the planted \keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal}; the iid band did
        not.
  \item \textbf{It earns that width.} Refit on the \nbSevenNPanels{} panels of
        \cref{sec:sc:valid}, the repaired interval covers the planted total \pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone}
        of the time against the iid fit's \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov}, level with the design-based
        randomization interval's \pc{\nbSevenRiCov}. Its per-week band covers at
        \pc{\nbSevenWeekCovArone}. Recovery is unchanged ($\nbSevenBiasArone$, EUR 000, of bias
        against $\nbSevenSeedBias$), and the seed-to-seed spread it must price is
        \keur{\nbSevenSeedSdArone}.
\end{enumerate}

An interval that is right \pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone} of the time where its predecessor was right
\pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} of the time is the difference between an interval and a decoration, bought
with one parameter and no new data. A posterior returns exactly the uncertainty its likelihood
encodes; give the likelihood the persistence the errors actually have, and the interval it returns
is one the decision can use.

The transferable rule is not ``always use AR(1)'', and it is worth being precise about \emph{why}
AR(1) works here, because in one respect it is the wrong family. The diagnosed error is \emph{flat}:
its autocorrelation does not decay (\cref{fig:sc:acf}), which is the signature of a level shift. A
stationary AR(1)'s autocorrelation \emph{does} decay geometrically, so at the posterior
$\rho = \nbSevenRhoPost$ it has all but vanished by lag eight, precisely the decay the data refused.
What AR(1) matches is therefore not the \emph{shape} of the autocorrelation but the \emph{variance of
the sum}: with $\rho$ pulled up, the summed variance inflates enough to cover honestly. It helps to
put the candidate error shapes on one axis, because they price the sum very differently: independent
errors give the total a variance in $n$, a \emph{constant} offset (the caricature the diagnosis used)
gives $n^2$, and a near-integrated random walk gives $n^3$. The flat autocorrelation really points at
that last, local-level case, so the truth sits between $n^2$ and $n^3$. AR(1) matches \emph{none} of
the shapes exactly. What it prices is the \emph{sum}: $\rho$ is pulled up until the summed variance
comes out right on this $n = \nbSevenNPost$ panel. A random-walk or local-level component would match the shape itself
(the route of \citet{brodersen2015}, taken up in \cref{sec:sc:sts}); AR(1) is the pragmatic
one-parameter stand-in that lands the total's scale here.
\hyperref[app:sc:acf]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.11} works through that gap between getting the scale right
and the shape wrong. The rule is: \emph{when a
posterior's interval under-covers, suspect the specification, and check the coverage}: the
likelihood first, as here, and any silently mis-scaled default prior next
(\refiv{} meets exactly that second case). The classical robustness
toolkit, clustered and HAC standard errors \citep{newey1987, bertrand2004}, the bootstrap, and
randomization inference, is built to be agnostic about the error structure, measuring it rather
than assuming it. \Citet{bertrand2004} made exactly this point for difference-in-differences, and
it cost the literature a decade of overstated significance to absorb: serially correlated errors,
summed over time, destroy naive standard errors. Here the same lesson arrives in Bayesian dress.
That is why the design-based interval is worth keeping alongside the posterior as its
\textbf{referee}: it committed to less, so it cannot be wrong about the autocorrelation, and it is
what flags a likelihood that is under-pricing its own uncertainty.

\subsection*{Does the AR(1) repair change the decision? No}

It is worth stating plainly what the repair did and did not touch, because the rollout call must not
appear to hinge on it. \textbf{It did not change the decision.} \Cref{sec:sc:classical} already
reached the verdict from the classical point estimate and the randomization interval, neither of which
involves a likelihood. What the repair changed is the \emph{posterior's width}, so that when
\cref{sec:sc:euro} reads a probability off it, that probability is honest rather than borrowed.

The same call survives every arm the chapter fits, and that is the point:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*, itemsep=.2em]
  \item \textbf{classical} (\cref{sec:sc:classical}): the profit interval straddles the cost, so this
        is not a confident buy;
  \item \textbf{shipped AR(1) posterior}: $\Prob(\text{profit} > c) = \nbSevenPGo$, a
        \textbf{\nbSevenDecision};
  \item \textbf{that same posterior re-priced} at the wider design-based scale (\keur{\nbSevenPlaceboSd}
        against its own \keur{\nbSevenBayesSd}): the probability rises only to \nbSevenPGoRescaled{}, a
        \textbf{\nbSevenDecisionRescaled} at most;
  \item the discarded \textbf{iid posterior} happens to print nearly the same probability here,
        because the break-even sits well below the bulk of both posteriors; agreement at one price is a
        coincidence of geometry, not a certificate, and the width, not the number, is what got it
        discarded.
\end{itemize}
Every one of them says the same thing: not a national rollout on this evidence. The label survives
every re-pricing, \nbSevenDecision{} on its own scale and \nbSevenDecisionRescaled{} on the design's,
because the campaign sits far enough from its break-even that no defensible choice of width reaches
the $0.9$ bar. That the number moves while the label does not is what scale-robustness looks like; a
campaign priced at its break-even would enjoy no such stability, and the check must be run, not
assumed.

So the division of labour: the posterior states the decision as a single
probability, and design-based inference certifies that probability is honest. Neither arm is
strictly needed to \emph{act} here; the classical arm alone already says hold. Together they do
more. The posterior turns a straddling interval into a probability a board can read. Design-based
inference certifies it: it never wrote a likelihood, so it had none to get wrong, and it is what
caught the iid fit under-pricing its own uncertainty and confirms the repaired width is honest.

% =====================================================================================
\section{The decision, in euros}
\label{sec:sc:euro}

\Cref{sec:sc:classical} already reached the rollout call on the classical evidence alone: at a
\pc{\nbSevenMargin} margin the lift of \keur{\nbSevenClTotal} clears its \keur{\nbSevenBreakevenLift}
break-even with room to spare (an iROAS of \nbSevenIroas{} against \nbSevenBreakevenIroas{}), and the
design-based interval carried through the margin runs from \keur{\nbSevenClProfitLo} to
\keur{\nbSevenClProfitHi}, straddling the \keur{\nbSevenCost} cost. This section states that same call
the way the posterior states it, as a single probability, and the two agree.

The decision quantity is the \emph{profit} the lift earns, $\text{profit} = \pc{\nbSevenMargin} \times \tau$,
and the rule is the book's standard convention, now applied to the repaired posterior of
\cref{sec:sc:compare} (the fit whose interval survived \cref{sec:sc:valid}'s coverage check):
%
\begin{equation}
