  \Prob(\text{profit} > c)\ \begin{cases}
    > 0.9 & \Rightarrow\ \textsc{go} \quad\text{(act without buying more evidence)},\\
    < 0.5 & \Rightarrow\ \textsc{no-go} \quad\text{(more likely than not it does not pay)},\\
    \text{otherwise} & \Rightarrow\ \textsc{test further}.
  \end{cases}
  \label{eq:sc:rule}
\end{equation}
%
At $c = \keur{\nbSevenCost}$ the posterior gives $\Prob(\text{profit} > c) = \nbSevenPGo$, an expected
profit of \keur{\nbSevenExpProfit} against the \keur{\nbSevenCost} spent (an expected net of
\keur{\nbSevenExpNet} and a \pc{\nbSevenExpRoi} return). The verdict is \textbf{\nbSevenDecision}
(\cref{fig:sc:decision}), the same action \cref{sec:sc:classical} reached, now carrying a probability
the straddling interval could not report.

\input{build/floats/nb07_euro}

Two things are true at once here, and conflating them is how marginal campaigns get scaled. The
placebo test says the effect is \emph{real} ($p = \nbSevenPRatio$). The economics say it
\emph{probably, but not provably, paid}. The expected profit, \keur{\nbSevenExpProfit}, clears the
\keur{\nbSevenCost} paid by \keur{\nbSevenExpNet}, a \pc{\nbSevenExpRoi} return, so on the average
the campaign is a clear winner. But the posterior is wide, and the probability that actually decides
the rollout, $\Prob(\text{profit} > c) = \nbSevenPGo$, says roughly one world in five still ends with
the spend unrecovered. This is exactly why the rule reads a \emph{probability} rather than a point
estimate: a healthy mean can coexist with a one-in-five chance of losing the budget, and only the
probability displays both facts at once. On the number that governs the decision the verdict is
\textbf{\nbSevenDecision}. Statistical significance and commercial worthiness are different questions
asked of different objects, and this campaign answers the first one yes and the second one with
four-in-five confidence, where the rule demands nine-in-ten.

The right-hand panel of \cref{fig:sc:decision} prices the gap rather than merely deploring it.
Sweeping a hypothetical price $c$ and plotting $\Prob(\text{profit} > c)$ gives a headroom curve, and
its crossing of the $0.9$ bar sits at \keur{\nbSevenHeadroom}. That is the highest price at which this
same evidence would already be a confident \textsc{go}, and the campaign was bought for
\keur{\nbSevenCost}, above it. The finding to take into the media-buying negotiation is therefore not
``the campaign failed'' but ``at a \pc{\nbSevenMargin} margin this evidence supports a confident buy
only up to \keur{\nbSevenHeadroom}, and the asking price was \keur{\nbSevenCost}'', a far more useful
deliverable than a yes or a no.

\subsection*{A \textsc{test further} still has a price tag}

A \textsc{test further} is a verdict about this campaign at this price, not a full stop, and it must
not end as a shrug either. This is the number the cost sweep of \cref{sec:sc:classical} could only
point at: at the interior prices where the interval still straddles the cost, better evidence has a
value the classical arm could not name. The deliverable is still an answer to
\emph{what evidence would settle this, and is it worth buying?} The value of perfect information,
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{VOI} \;=\; \E\big[\max(\text{profit} - c,\ 0)\big] \;-\; \max\big(\E[\text{profit}] - c,\ 0\big),
  \label{eq:sc:voi}
\end{equation}
%
is the expected gain from deciding \emph{after} the uncertainty resolves rather than acting now on the
mean \citep{raiffa1961} (\hyperref[app:sc:voi]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.12} works it through).
It comes to \keur{\nbSevenVoi}, and one subtlety in that number is worth
naming, because the chapter runs two decision criteria at once. \Cref{eq:sc:voi} takes the risk-neutral
baseline, ``act on the mean'': since expected profit (\keur{\nbSevenExpProfit}) sits \keur{\nbSevenExpNet}
\emph{above} cost, that baseline is to \emph{invest}, and perfect information (which commits only in the
worlds where profit actually clears cost) beats it by \keur{\nbSevenVoi}. The rollout rule of
\cref{eq:sc:rule} is stricter and deliberately risk-averse: it demands $\Prob(\text{profit} > c) > 0.9$,
far more than a merely positive mean, so it \emph{holds}. Against that stricter hold, the value of
resolving the uncertainty is larger. It grows by exactly the \keur{\nbSevenExpNet} the
act-on-the-mean baseline would have banked. Either way the conclusion is the same: the value is
small, because most of the posterior's mass already sits above the cost line and perfect information
would rarely overturn the commitment. Either
figure is an upper bound on any confirmatory-research budget: a study costing many times \keur{\nbSevenVoi}
to resolve a \keur{\nbSevenVoi} question is not a cautious decision, it is an expensive one.

Sizing that test completes the picture, and it answers a different question than the VOI does. A
second geo test treating $k$ independent markets tightens the posterior roughly as $1/\sqrt{k}$, and
because the profit posterior is centred \emph{above} the cost line, tightening moves mass towards
$\{\text{profit} > c\}$. The centre clears the price by \keur{\nbSevenExpNet}, so the $0.9$ bar is
reached after only a factor-two shrink of the width: the sizing routine returns
$k \approx \nbSevenKNeeded$ treated markets. Read that as a lower bound, because the arithmetic
flatters the test twice over, treating the $k$ market-level estimates as independent when they share
the same macro factors, and taking the per-market scale from a posterior the multi-seed check calls
optimistic. So the test is \emph{feasible}, and the VOI just said it is not \emph{worth funding}, and
both are true because they price information for different decisions. The \keur{\nbSevenVoi} is what
certainty is worth for this \keur{\nbSevenCost} media buy; a $k$-market test is bought to de-risk the
national rollout, whose stakes, not this campaign's, are what pay for it. Keep the two ledgers
separate and both sentences fit in one memo: do not fund a test to re-litigate this buy, and do not
roll out without one. What remains at this price is to renegotiate (the headroom curve says a
confident buy holds up to \keur{\nbSevenHeadroom}), to run the confirmatory test against the rollout
ledger, or to accept the four-in-five bet with open eyes. What is not on the list is a blind national
rollout.

% =====================================================================================
\section{The more precise route: structural time series}
\label{sec:sc:sts}

The AR(1) repair was honest about its own limitation: it prices the \emph{sum} correctly while
getting the error's \emph{shape} wrong (\cref{fig:sc:acf}). The flat autocorrelation points at a
local-level, near-integrated error, a level that drifts like a random walk rather than a shock that
decays. A model built from that shape would fit a state-space \emph{level} component jointly with the
donor blend, so the counterfactual would carry a random-walk uncertainty that widens with the horizon
by construction, instead of an AR(1) whose $\rho$ has been pulled up until the summed variance happens
to come out right. That is the route of \citet{brodersen2015}, and it is the shape-correct one.

This chapter did not take it, deliberately. The extra machinery, a latent level fit alongside the
weights, buys shape-correctness that does not move \emph{this} verdict: on a \nbSevenNPost-week window
AR(1) already lands the total's scale and covers at \pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone}, and the rollout call
was settled classically before any likelihood was written. Paying for the state-space model here
would sharpen a number the decision does not turn on.

Where it would earn its keep is the honest upper bound. If you needed the error model to be
\emph{right} rather than merely \emph{sufficient}, a longer post-period is the case that forces it.
A constant offset charges the total $n^2$; a genuine random-walk level charges $n^3$. The gap between
the two widens with the window, so over a long horizon the inflated-$\rho$ approximation would drift
away from honest coverage where a local-level model would hold it. That is the ceiling, and where it
sits. It is a pointer, not a result: no state-space model is fitted in this chapter, and none of its
numbers appear.

% =====================================================================================
\section{What can go wrong}
\label{sec:sc:wrong}

Three failure modes matter in practice, and this section demonstrates each on the panel rather than
describing it: the naive shortcuts that book the shared wave as campaign effect, anticipation that
contaminates the launch date, and spillover, the one violation no placebo can see. The first two are
caught by tests the chapter has already built. The third is caught by nothing, which is why it ends
in a design rule rather than a diagnostic.

\subsection*{The naive estimators}

\Cref{tab:sc:naive} runs the shortcuts of \cref{sec:sc:decision} on the same panel, against a true
weekly lift of \keur{\nbSevenTrueAvg}. Before-and-after books whatever the shared factors did
between the two windows, and in this panel they moved \emph{against} the campaign, so it comes
in at \keur{\nbSevenNaiveBa}, roughly half the truth. That is worth dwelling on, because the folk
version of the bias has the naive estimator always flattering the campaign. It does not: the trend
bias has no fixed sign. It simply has nothing to do with the advertising, and an analyst who
``knows'' which way it errs is guessing. Treated-minus-average-control errs in the opposite
direction, at \keur{\nbSevenNaiveAvgctrl}, because it differences nothing out and carries the raw
level gap between a large metro and an average one. A \emph{difference-in-differences} removes both
problems at once: take the change in the treated market and subtract the change in the control, so
anything that moved both markets cancels (\refdid{} treats it in full). It does markedly better.
But a residual bias survives, because the average control
has the wrong factor loadings and so does not cancel the common wave cleanly. Only the
\emph{reweighted} control lands near the truth. The error column of \cref{tab:sc:naive} ranks them
without commentary; the reweighted estimator's error is smaller than the next best by a
factor of several, and \cref{fig:sc:naivebars} plots the four against the planted truth. Same data,
four estimates: the estimator \emph{is} the identification strategy.

\input{build/tables/nb07_naive}
\input{build/floats/nb07_naivebars}

\subsection*{Anticipation}

\Cref{as:sc:anticip} is tested by moving the launch backwards, the \emph{placebo-in-time} test that
\hyperref[app:sc:placebotimeapp]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.7} formalises. Refitting with a fake launch in week
\nbSevenPlaceboTimeWeek{} (inside the true pre-period, where by construction nothing happened)
must return an effect of approximately zero, and does: \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboTimeGap} per week,
comfortably inside the pre-fit noise of \keur{\nbSevenClPreRmse}. The yardstick is the classical
fit's, and deliberately so: the fake-launch gap is itself an SLSQP refit, so it must be graded
against the SLSQP fitter's own pre-fit RMSE. The shipped posterior's \keur{\nbSevenBayesPreRmse} is
context here, not the ruler. The method does not invent lift where there is none, as
\cref{fig:sc:placebotime} shows.

\input{build/floats/nb07_placebotime}

\subsection*{Spillover, the assumption no placebo can see}

\Cref{as:sc:sutva} is the one that placebo inference structurally cannot test: if the campaign
leaks into neighbouring markets, then every placebo refit is contaminated in exactly the same way
and nothing looks unusual. The simulator can do what no real dataset can, and plant the violation.
A fraction $\varphi$ of the true lift is leaked into \nbSevenSpNNeighbours{} neighbouring donors,
$Y_{jt} \mathrel{+}= \varphi\,\Delta_t$, and the estimator is refit.

The first-order prediction, before running it, is that the contaminated donors rise, so the
synthetic rises, so the measured gap \emph{shrinks} by roughly the spilled weight:
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{bias} \;\approx\; -\,\varphi \sum_{j \in \text{neighbours}} w_j \;\times\; \tau .
  \label{eq:sc:spill}
\end{equation}
%
At $\varphi = \nbSevenSpPhi$, with \pc{\nbSevenSpNeighbourWeight} of the weight on the
contaminated donors, \cref{eq:sc:spill} predicts a bias of $\nbSevenSpPredicted$ (EUR 000) and
the refit delivers $\nbSevenSpMeasured$ (\cref{tab:sc:spillover}), and \cref{fig:sc:spillover} traces
the attenuation as $\varphi$ grows.

\input{build/tables/nb07_spillover}
\input{build/floats/nb07_spillover_curve}

Two consequences follow, and the first is more reassuring than most analysts expect.
\textbf{Spillover attenuates}: synthetic control \emph{under}-states a real campaign, never
inflates it. It is conservative for a \textsc{go} call, though it can certainly turn a true
\textsc{go} into a \textsc{no-go}. And because no placebo can flag it, \textbf{the fix is design,
not statistics}: excluding the suspect donors up front restores the estimate to
\keur{\nbSevenSpExcluded}, against \keur{\nbSevenSpContaminated} for the contaminated pool and
\keur{\nbSevenSpClean} for the clean one. A smaller clean donor pool beats a larger contaminated
one. On real data, drop adjacent and media-overlapping markets from the pool before fitting
anything.

\subsection*{The limits}

Three remain, and they are properties of the design rather than defects of the code. \emph{One
treated unit buys limited power}: with \nbSevenNDmas{} markets, the permutation $p$-value is
granular and cannot resolve below roughly $1/\nbSevenNDmas$ however strong the effect. \emph{The
design sits near the edge of its own resolution}, as \cref{sec:sc:model} measured: the decision-carrying
fit clears the pre-fit gate, but the margin is thin, and the week-to-week wobble is of the same order
as the weekly lift it must detect. A longer pre-period or a
richer donor pool is the real fix, and the \textsc{test further} of \cref{sec:sc:euro} should be read in that
light. And
\emph{the estimator is high-variance here}, as \cref{sec:sc:valid} measured, which is the reason
this chapter leads its conclusions with the placebo test rather than with any interval.

% =====================================================================================
\section{On real data}
\label{sec:sc:real}

Everything above was graded against a truth that was planted. The obvious objection (that a
method which recovers a lift someone put there is not thereby proved to work) deserves a real
answer, and there is one. A real advertiser spent \kusd{\nbSevenBSpend} on a real campaign in
\nbSevenBNTreated{} US geos over \nbSevenBCampaignDays{} days in early 2015, leaving
\nbSevenBNControl{} geos dark. The daily sales-and-cost panel is published with Google's geo
experiment methodology \citep{vaver2011, kerman2017}. It is exactly the shape of panel every
advertiser's warehouse already holds: \nbSevenBNGeos{} units, \nbSevenBNDaysTotal{} days,
one campaign window, and \nbSevenBCooldownDays{} days of cooldown after the money stops.

Three things replace the planted truth, one per subsection below. First, a \emph{referee}: because
the geos were randomized, a model-free difference-in-differences gives an unbiased benchmark that
every model-based estimate must land inside. Second, an \emph{exam}: does synthetic control land
there, do its diagnostics pass, and does it refuse the one market it cannot match? Third, an
\emph{off-switch}: the campaign ends, so a genuine effect must collapse when the spend stops.

\subsection*{The referee: what randomization buys}

The \nbSevenBNGeos{} geos were split into treatment and control \emph{at random}, in
\nbSevenBNPairs{} size-matched pairs. That design licenses an estimator that fits no model at
all: a matched-pairs \emph{difference-in-differences} (\refdid{}). Take each pair's treated geo,
take its change in mean daily sales from the pre-window to the
campaign window, subtract the same change for its matched control partner, and average over the
pairs:
%
\begin{equation}
  d_p \;=\; \big(\bar Y^{\,\text{tr}}_{p,\text{post}} - \bar Y^{\,\text{tr}}_{p,\text{pre}}\big)
          - \big(\bar Y^{\,\text{ct}}_{p,\text{post}} - \bar Y^{\,\text{ct}}_{p,\text{pre}}\big),
  \qquad
  \hat\tau_{\text{DiD}} \;=\; N T \cdot \frac{1}{P}\sum_{p=1}^{P} d_p ,
  \label{eq:sc:did}
\end{equation}
%
with $N = \nbSevenBNTreated$ geos, $T = \nbSevenBCampaignDays$ days and $P = \nbSevenBNPairs$
pairs, and a standard error built from the scatter of the pair differences,
$NT \cdot s_d / \sqrt{P}$. The covariance choice is not a default: randomization happened at the
geo level within pairs, so the pair differences are the design's own unit of variability. A
geo-day-level iid standard error would treat thousands of correlated geo-days as independent
draws and report an interval several times too narrow: \citeauthor{bertrand2004}'s warning,
one chapter later and in a different costume. Pairing is itself variance reduction. The geo size
factor (the largest geo sells \nbSevenBSizeRatio{} times the smallest) differences out
\emph{within} each pair, and ignoring the pairs inflates the standard error from
\kusd{\nbSevenBDidSe} to \kusd{\nbSevenBUnpairedSe}, a factor of \nbSevenBPairingGain{}.
\Cref{fig:sc:realpairs} shows the pair differences themselves, the raw material of the benchmark,
and \hyperref[app:sc:pairs]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.15} works the estimator through.

\input{build/floats/nb07b_pairs}

\Cref{eq:sc:did} is \emph{unbiased by construction}. It is the closest thing to truth that real
data ever hands anyone, and it is what replaces the planted lift: every model-based estimate must
land inside its interval. It gives \kusd{\nbSevenBDidTotal} of incremental sales, 90\,\% interval
$[\kusd{\nbSevenBDidLo},\ \kusd{\nbSevenBDidHi}]$, an incremental return on ad spend of
\nbSevenBDidIroas{}. Note the width. Unbiasedness is not precision, and that width, nearly
half the effect in each direction, is the entire commercial reason to fit a model on top of a
perfectly good experiment.

One provenance note keeps the benchmark honest. Google's own methodology papers analyse this class
of experiment with time-based regression \citep{kerman2017}, and a public replication of Google's
reference implementation on this panel lands at the same sign and order as the matched-pairs anchor
above. That readout is a replication of reference code, not a figure the paper itself prints (its
worked example is a different, anonymized experiment), so the chapter cites it as corroboration and
grades everything that follows against the randomized anchor computed here.

\subsection*{The contestant, and the exam}

The classical synthetic control of \cref{sec:sc:classical} is applied unchanged. The treated unit
is now the \emph{cell}, the mean daily sales per treated geo, so that the target stays inside
the donors' convex hull (\cref{as:sc:hull}). It tracks the cell to a pre-fit RMSE of
\usd{\nbSevenBScPreRmse} per geo per day on a level of \usd{\nbSevenBPreLevel}, or
\pc{\nbSevenBScPreRmsePct} (\cref{fig:sc:realsc}). It returns \kusd{\nbSevenBScTotal}:
$\nbSevenBScErrVsAnchor$ (USD 000) from the randomized referee, or \nbSevenBScAnchorSes{} anchor
standard errors, comfortably inside its interval. The Bayesian fit of \cref{sec:sc:bayes} returns
\kusd{\nbSevenBBayesTotal}, \nbSevenBBayesAnchorSes{} anchor standard errors from the referee.
\Cref{tab:sc:realcompare} records all three.

Before trusting that fit, the pre-period offers a plausibility check the simulator never needed.
\Cref{tab:sc:realbalance} sets the treated cell beside the donor geos on level, pre-trend,
volatility and correlation, so the convex-hull premise (\cref{as:sc:hull}) can be checked by eye
rather than taken on faith.

\input{build/tables/nb07b_walkthrough_balance}

\input{build/floats/nb07b_sc}
\input{build/tables/nb07b_compare}
\input{build/floats/nb07b_posterior}

\Cref{fig:sc:realposterior} is the picture this section is really about: the shipped posterior over
the dollar total (the AR(1) fit argued for in \cref{sec:sc:compare} and detailed below), with the
randomized anchor falling squarely inside its 90\,\% band. All three estimates land inside the
anchor's interval (\cref{tab:sc:realcompare}); the model agrees with the experiment. Two things are
then left, and they are the next two subsections: trust the sampler that drew the posterior, and
price its width.

The sampler's diagnostics are reported here rather than waved through.
The chains converge ($\hat R = \nbSevenBRhat$ and a minimum bulk effective sample size of
\nbSevenBEss{}). A \emph{divergent transition} is the one warning that can invalidate a posterior
while every other diagnostic looks immaculate, so both fits' counts are stated: \nbSevenBDivergences{}
on the fit of \cref{eq:sc:lik} and \nbSevenBArDivergences{} on the shipped AR(1) fit.
The question a non-zero count raises is whether the geometry the sampler struggled
with has moved the answer, and that question has an answer rather than a reassurance. The same
objective admits a deterministic, divergence-free solution, the SLSQP fit of
\cref{eq:sc:slsqp}, and the two land \kusd{\nbSevenBBayesMoved} apart, which is
\pc{\nbSevenBBayesMovedPct} of the estimate and a small fraction of the posterior's own interval
width; the shipped posterior additionally brackets the randomized anchor
(\cref{fig:sc:realposterior}). Divergences on this model live at the corners of the simplex, where
the Dirichlet's density pinches
against a boundary that a donor's weight is being pushed towards; they cost sampling efficiency,
not location. A cross-check of that kind is what a divergence count obliges an analyst to produce,
and it is cheaper than the fit that raised the alarm.

That is the exam, and it is the only exam real data can set: the method passes it. And the same
bake-off runs, with no simulator, on a single real market. Pretending only one mid-size geo had
been treated reproduces \cref{tab:sc:naive}'s failures exactly (\cref{tab:sc:realnaive}), including
one the simulation could only warn about. The largest geo in the country is bigger than every
control geo and therefore lies \emph{outside} its own donor hull. \Cref{as:sc:hull} fails, the
simplex does its honest best by piling all its weight on the single largest donor, and the pre-fit
gate \textbf{fails loudly}: a pre-RMSE of \usd{\nbSevenBHullPreRmse} against a bar of
\usd{\nbSevenBHullGateRef} (\cref{fig:sc:hullfail}). The estimate it \emph{would} have shipped implies an iROAS of
\nbSevenBHullIroas{} against the experiment's \nbSevenBDidIroas{}, precisely the inflated
number an unscrupulous deck would have quoted. The method's own diagnostic refuses it. That
self-diagnosis is worth more than the estimate.

\input{build/tables/nb07b_naive}
\input{build/floats/nb07b_hull_fail}

\subsection*{The off-switch}

Real data affords one falsification that no simulator provides for free: \textbf{the campaign
ends}. The spend stops, and a genuine advertising effect must collapse when the money does. It
does (\cref{fig:sc:realgap}): a sustained $\nbSevenBPostGap$ dollars per geo per day through the
paid window, and $\nbSevenBCoolGap$ dollars per geo per day in the \nbSevenBCooldownDays-day
cooldown. An effect that persisted after the spend stopped would smell of a confound rather than a
campaign. This one switches off, which simultaneously certifies the attribution and tells the
media planner there is no carryover annuity to book. The placebo apparatus of
\cref{sec:sc:classical} agrees. On \nbSevenBNPseudo{} pseudo-cells of untreated geos, the real
cell is the most extreme unit on the RMSE ratio ($p = \nbSevenBPRatio$) and second most extreme
on the raw gap ($p = \nbSevenBPGap$). And a fake launch inside the pre-period finds
$\nbSevenBPlaceboTimeGap$ dollars per geo per day: \pc{\nbSevenBPlaceboTimePct} of the real
effect, and the wrong sign.

\input{build/floats/nb07b_gap}

\subsection*{How wide is the interval the dollar decision will use?}

Fresh worlds are not available here, so coverage cannot be \emph{measured}. But it need not be
guessed either. The placebos re-run \emph{the same estimator} under a known null, and that is the
fair referee for the posterior's width. \Cref{tab:sc:realscales} sets the scales against each
other. The iid fit of \cref{eq:sc:lik} (the same likelihood, applied to the same estimator, on
real data) puts the standard deviation of the window total at \kusd{\nbSevenBBayesSd}. The
placebo distribution of the same estimator gives \kusd{\nbSevenBPlaceboSd}. A half-size
pseudo-cell is noisier than the real cell, so deflating for that gives a conservative floor of
\kusd{\nbSevenBPlaceboSdFloor}. The iid posterior is therefore too tight by
\nbSevenBRatioPlaceboFloor--\nbSevenBRatioPlacebo$\times$.

\input{build/tables/nb07b_scales}

That is \cref{sec:sc:compare}'s finding, reproduced on real data, in the same direction, with the
same cause. What was an imported claim from a simulator is now a measured one.

One thing this does \emph{not} say, and the distinction is worth the sentence. The randomized
anchor's standard error is \nbSevenBRatioAnchor$\times$ the posterior's, and that ratio is
\textbf{not} the criticism. An unweighted matched-pairs difference-in-differences is a genuinely
noisier estimator than a donor-matched synthetic control. Cutting that variance is the entire job
of the model, and it really does cut it. The fair referee for the posterior's width is the placebo
distribution of the \emph{same} estimator, and that referee returns a verdict of roughly two, not
eight. Both statements hold at once: \textbf{synthetic control is far more precise than the raw
experiment, and the posterior is still overconfident about how precise.}

\subsection*{The dollar decision, and the margin trap}

This subsection splits the dollar decision into its two questions: does the campaign pay, and is the
interval that says so honest? The first is the margin trap of \cref{sec:sc:euro}, now in dollars. An
iROAS of three is not a threefold return: ad spend buys \emph{revenue}, and the firm keeps only the
gross margin on it. The campaign pays if
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{iROAS} \times \text{margin} \;>\; 1
  \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
  \text{iROAS} \;>\; \frac{1}{\text{margin}} ,
  \label{eq:sc:margin}
\end{equation}
%
so at the assumed \pc{\nbSevenBMargin} gross margin the bar is not $1.0$ but
\nbSevenBBreakEvenIroas{}, and the posterior sits almost exactly on top of it. The break-even
margin is $1/\text{iROAS} = \pc{\nbSevenBBreakEvenMargin}$, and the campaign reaches 90\,\%
confidence of profitability only above \pc{\nbSevenBMarginNinety} (\cref{fig:sc:realmargin}). The
lift is beyond doubt; the profitability turns entirely on a finance input the model cannot see.

\input{build/floats/nb07b_margincurve}

And the tension of \cref{sec:sc:compare} arrives here too, in dollars. The decision above is taken
on the \emph{shipped} posterior (the AR(1) fit, the one that survived the diagnostics), which
puts $\Prob(\text{profitable}) = \nbSevenBPProfitAr$ at the assumed margin. The discarded iid fit
would have reported \nbSevenBPProfit{} for the same campaign on the same data. The same discipline
that governs \cref{sec:sc:valid} governs the euros here, and a likelihood that under-prices its own
uncertainty is not the one to price the decision. That gap is not academic: it is the difference
between a call a board would barely question and one it would interrogate.

The re-pricing ladder makes the point sharper still. Take the iid posterior and, re-centring
nothing, merely re-price it at the placebo floor: its confidence falls to \nbSevenBPProfitFloor{};
at the raw placebo scale, \nbSevenBPProfitRaw{}; at the anchor's, \nbSevenBPProfitAnchor{}
(\cref{tab:sc:realscales}, last column). The call survives at every scale
(\nbSevenBDecision{}), because at a \pc{\nbSevenBMargin} margin the campaign is on a knife-edge that
no re-scaling rescues. But under the iid likelihood the confidence attached to it was borrowed,
not earned. A re-pricing step can expose borrowed confidence, not replace it: the patched number
comes from outside the model, quotable in a memo and impossible to propagate.

The AR(1) likelihood of \cref{eq:sc:ar1lik}, fitted to this panel with the same donors and windows,
needs no patch. Its posterior on the residual autocorrelation is centred at \nbSevenBArRho{}. The
persistence is there in the real data, exactly as it was in the simulated panel. And it re-prices
the iid standard deviation by a factor of \nbSevenBArVsIid{}, from \kusd{\nbSevenBBayesSd} to
\kusd{\nbSevenBArSd}, moving the estimate not at all (\kusd{\nbSevenBArTotal}, against the anchor's
\kusd{\nbSevenBDidTotal}, with a 90\,\% interval of
$[\kusd{\nbSevenBArLo},\ \kusd{\nbSevenBArHi}]$ that brackets the randomized anchor,
\cref{fig:sc:realposterior}). Now grade that width against the design: the
corrected likelihood's own standard deviation sits at \nbSevenBArVsFloor$\times$ the conservative
placebo floor. It has arrived, from a likelihood alone and with no placebo in sight, at the
uncertainty scale the randomization itself implies. That is the whole claim of
\cref{sec:sc:compare}, out of the simulator and on a real advertiser's panel. The width was never
a fact about Bayesian inference; it was a fact about the error model. And a model that told the truth
about the errors prices its own uncertainty the way the design does.

One comparison closes the section, because a reader following both halves will ask how the two
verdicts relate. The simulated campaign is a \nbSevenDecision{} ($\Prob(\text{profit} > c) =
\nbSevenPGo$); the real one is a \nbSevenBDecision{} ($\nbSevenBPProfitAr$). Same method, same margin
trap, an iROAS near three in both, and this time the same label, yet the two probabilities have
opposite anatomies, which is what makes the comparison instructive. The simulated campaign clears its
price by a wide margin on a \emph{wide} posterior; the real one clears its price by a sliver on a
\emph{tight} one. The real ``unit'' is the \emph{cell}, the average over \nbSevenBNTreated{} treated
geos, so its posterior standard deviation is a far smaller share of the total (\kusd{\nbSevenBArSd} on
\kusd{\nbSevenBArTotal}) than the single simulated market's (\keur{\nbSevenBayesSd} on
\keur{\nbSevenBayesTotal}). Aggregation across markets buys precision; what it cannot buy is a wider
victory margin, which only the economics set. A probability quoted without its anatomy hides exactly
this, so the deliverable should carry the margin and the width next to the $P$ they produce, which is
also the practical reason a real geo test splits many markets into arms rather than lighting up a
single one.

\begin{remark}[Why grade the method on an experiment]
The randomized experiment did not merely validate a number. It validated \emph{the method that
will be used when there is no experiment}. The moment a firm has one treated market chosen by a
media planner rather than a coin, everything in this section except the anchor disappears, and
synthetic control is the only member of the family still standing. That is why the chapter grades
it here, where the answer is known.
\end{remark}

% =====================================================================================
\section{Takeaways}
\label{sec:sc:take}

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, itemsep=.45em]
  \item \textbf{The counterfactual is built, not found.} With no control group, the estimator
        \emph{is} the identification strategy. A weighted blend of donors, constrained to the
        simplex so it cannot extrapolate, reconstructs the treated market's untreated path from
        the factors it shares with everyone else. The naive shortcuts do not fail by a little
        (\cref{tab:sc:naive}); they fail by booking the shared macro wave as campaign effect.

  \item \textbf{The pre-fit is a gate, not a formality.} If the synthetic cannot track the
        treated unit \emph{before} launch, the post-launch gap means nothing. The fit that carries
        the decision clears it here (classical pre-RMSE \keur{\nbSevenClPreRmse} against the
        \keur{\nbSevenGateRef} bar); the looser regularized blend does not
        (\keur{\nbSevenBayesPreRmse}), which is a reason to read the width cautiously, not to
        distrust the number. Where the gate truly bites is a market no blend can match: on real data
        it stops the country's largest market (outside its own donor hull) from shipping an iROAS of
        \nbSevenBHullIroas{} against a true \nbSevenBDidIroas{}.

  \item \textbf{Classical and Bayesian agree on the number.} They differ by
        \pc{\nbSevenEstDiffPct} of the effect (\keur{\nbSevenEstDiff} on an effect of
        \keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal}) and both undershoot the planted truth, as does the discarded
        iid fit. Neither is ``the better of the two'' in any sense worth reporting. The sampler
        adds no causal content; the identification does all of it. What the two arms do not share
        is the shape of the blend: \nbSevenClEffDonors{} effective donors against
        \nbSevenBayesEffDonors{}, which is a trade in interpretability, not in accuracy.

  \item \textbf{The iid interval on the total under-covers badly, and the reason is a
        misspecified likelihood, not the paradigm.} A nominal 90\,\% credible interval covers on
        \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} of fresh panels, against \pc{\nbSevenRiCov} for the design-based
        randomization interval. That posterior prices the standard deviation of the
        \nbSevenNPost-week total at \keur{\nbSevenIidSd} when the truth is \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd}.
        \Cref{eq:sc:n2} is the whole story, and its shape matters more than its size: the error is
        a \emph{persistent offset} plus weekly noise, so
        $\Var(\sum e_t) = n^{2}\sigma_\omega^{2} + n\,\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}$. The $n^{2}$ rides on
        the \emph{offset's} variance, and does \textbf{not} require the autocorrelation to be
        large. It is not (\nbSevenRhoOne{} at lag one). What it is, is \emph{flat}
        (\nbSevenRhoEight{} still, at lag eight), and a correlation that refuses to decay is the
        signature of a level shift. \Cref{eq:sc:lik} set every one of them to zero. Repair the
        likelihood or repair the posterior \citep[sandwich-corrected;][]{mueller2013}. Do not
        repair your faith in Bayes.

  \item \textbf{Repairing the likelihood repairs the interval, and costs one parameter.} Giving
        the residual an AR(1) law (\cref{eq:sc:ar1}; posterior $\rho = \nbSevenRhoPost$, 90\,\%
        interval $[\nbSevenRhoLo,\ \nbSevenRhoHi]$) and propagating it forward into the
        counterfactual widens the total-lift band by a factor of \nbSevenWidthRatio{}. That takes its
        coverage from \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} to \pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone} (level with the
        design-based interval's \pc{\nbSevenRiCov}) while nudging the estimate from the iid
        fit's \keur{\nbSevenIidTotal} up beside the classical arm's \keur{\nbSevenClTotal}.
        The same fix, applied to the real geo experiment of \cref{sec:sc:real}, lands at
        \nbSevenBArVsFloor$\times$ the placebo floor. The transferable rule is not ``always use
        AR(1)''; it is \emph{check the coverage, and suspect the specification when it fails}:
        here the fix was the likelihood; in \refiv{} it is a default prior.

  \item \textbf{The design-based arm is the referee, not the rival.} Randomization inference never
        wrote a likelihood, so it had no likelihood to get wrong. It measures the estimator's error
        distribution from units known to be untreated, persistent macro mismatch and all. It cost
        efficiency for that agnosticism (its interval is the widest in \cref{tab:sc:compare})
        and it kept its promise throughout, which is precisely how the broken likelihood was caught.
        Keep it on the payroll for the next one.

  \item \textbf{The posterior states the decision as a probability; the classical arm reaches the
        same call without one.} $\Prob(\text{profit} > \text{cost})$ has no classical counterpart, and
        \cref{eq:sc:rule} consumes nothing else, but the rollout \emph{action} was already settled in
        \cref{sec:sc:classical} by the point estimate and the randomization interval. So the
        discipline worth keeping is a division
        of labour, not a dependence: \textbf{state the decision as a probability from the posterior,
        and take the confidence in it from design-based inference}. The euro probability inherits
        whatever optimism the interval carries, which is why it is read off the repaired fit and never
        \cref{eq:sc:lik}'s; re-pricing that posterior at the design's own scale moves it from
        \nbSevenPGo{} to \nbSevenPGoRescaled{} and leaves the verdict where it was:
        \nbSevenDecisionRescaled{}.

  \item \textbf{Significant and profitable are different questions.} The effect here is real
        ($p = \nbSevenPRatio$) and it \emph{probably, but not provably}, cleared its price
        ($\Prob(\text{profit} > c) = \nbSevenPGo$, expected net value \keur{\nbSevenExpNet},
        \pc{\nbSevenExpRoi} return): \nbSevenDecision{}.
        Conflating the two is how marginal campaigns get scaled nationally. The headroom curve
        (this evidence clears the bar at a price of \keur{\nbSevenHeadroom}) is the more useful
        deliverable than either a $p$-value or a verdict.
\end{enumerate}

% =====================================================================================
\clearpage
\phantomsection
\section*{Appendix \thechapter.A\quad Formal machinery}
\label{app:sc:formal}
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Appendix \thechapter.A\quad Formal machinery}

This appendix carries the machinery the body points to but does not stop for: the classical optimiser
and the pre-fit statistic, the geometry of the simplex, the two priors, the two placebo tests and the
interval each yields, and then the graduate-level core the chapter turns on, which referee grades
which posterior, the AR(1) likelihood written in observables, and the persistent-offset error model
that explains the under-covering total. Every section is stated with the worked numbers from this
chapter's run and a figure that shows the concept.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.1\quad Constrained least squares, and SLSQP}
\label{app:sc:slsqp}
The classical fit solves \cref{eq:sc:slsqp}: minimise the pre-period sum of squared gaps over weights
on the simplex. The objective is quadratic and the feasible set is convex, so the minimum is unique
and there are no local traps. \emph{Sequential least-squares quadratic programming} (SLSQP) reaches it
by repeatedly replacing the objective with its local quadratic model and the constraints with their
linear model, solving that easy sub-problem, and iterating; on a convex quadratic it converges in a
handful of steps and a few milliseconds. \Cref{fig:sc:appslsqp} slices the objective along the edge
between the two heaviest donors and marks the optimum. Because the fit is deterministic and cheap, the
same solver carries every robustness check in the chapter: the placebo refits, the leave-one-out
drops, the launch-date sweep.
\input{build/floats/nb07_app_slsqp}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.2\quad The pre-period fit: RMSE}
\label{app:sc:rmse}
\Cref{eq:sc:rmse} defines the pre-period RMSE as the root-mean-square of the weekly gaps over the
weeks before launch: square each pre-launch miss, average over $t < T_0$, take the root. It is in the
units of the outcome, thousands of euros per market-week, so it reads directly as the typical weekly
tracking error. The classical fit reaches \keur{\nbSevenClPreRmse} here (\cref{fig:sc:apprmse}), and
the pre-fit gate of \cref{sec:sc:model} compares that against a third of the weekly lift the design
must detect. It is an RMSE, not a correlation or an $R^2$, because the decision cares about euros of
miss, not the share of variance explained.
\input{build/floats/nb07_app_rmse}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.3\quad The simplex and the convex hull}
\label{app:sc:simplex}
The constraint $w \in \simplex$ (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_simplex}) forces the synthetic into the
\emph{convex hull} of the donors: each synthetic value is a weighted average of real donor values,
weights non-negative and summing to one, so it can never leave the range the donors spanned that week.
That is interpolation, never extrapolation (\cref{fig:sc:simplex}). Dropping the constraint (ordinary
least squares) fits the pre-period more closely but extrapolates out of sample, which is why its
zero-lift placebo error is \nbSevenHullOosRatio$\times$ the simplex fit's. The constraint is paid for
in the weeks you can check and earns its keep in the weeks you cannot.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.4\quad The Dirichlet prior on the weights}
\label{app:sc:dirichlet}
The Bayesian fit needs a prior that lives on the simplex, and the Dirichlet is exactly that: every
draw is a set of non-negative weights summing to one. Its single concentration parameter $\alpha$
tunes the shape (\cref{fig:sc:appdirichlet}). At $\alpha = 1$ it is \emph{flat}, every blend equally
likely, which is the no-donor-favoured default the chapter uses; below one it favours the corners
(sparse blends on a few donors); above one it favours diffuse blends near the centre.
\Cref{fig:sc:priorsens} shows the posterior of the total lift barely moves across these choices, which
is why the prior is a detail here and the likelihood is the story.
\input{build/floats/nb07_app_dirichlet}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.5\quad The posterior-predictive counterfactual}
\label{app:sc:ppc}
The counterfactual for a post-launch week is not the mean blend but the posterior \emph{predictive} of
\cref{eq:sc:ppc}: the blend plus a fresh draw of that week's own noise $\varepsilon_t$. The
$\varepsilon_t$ term earns its place, because the treated market's untreated path would itself have
carried week-to-week noise; a band drawn from the mean blend alone carries the uncertainty in the
weights but not that noise, and is too narrow per week. \Cref{fig:sc:gap} shows the predictive band
hugging zero before launch and departing after it. That is the per-week uncertainty; the total's
uncertainty is the separate and harder story of \hyperref[app:sc:acf]{Appendix~\thechapter.A.11}.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.6\quad The permutation p-value}
\label{app:sc:perm}
With one treated unit there is no sampling distribution to read a standard error from, so inference
comes from \emph{permutation} (\cref{eq:sc:vocab_perm}): treat each donor in turn as if it had been
treated, refit, and collect the placebo test statistics. The p-value is the share of units, treated
plus placebos, whose statistic is at least the treated market's. Here the treated market ranks
\nbSevenTreatedRank{} of \nbSevenNUnits{} on the RMSE-ratio statistic, for $p = \nbSevenPRatio$
(\cref{fig:sc:placebo}). The $+1$ terms in \cref{eq:sc:vocab_perm} count the treated unit inside its
own reference set, the standard finite-sample correction that floors the smallest achievable p-value
at $1/(J{+}1)$ rather than at zero.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.7\quad Placebo-in-time}
\label{app:sc:placebotimeapp}
Placebo-in-space asks whether the treated \emph{unit} is unusual; placebo-in-\emph{time} asks whether
the treated \emph{date} is. Move the launch earlier, into the true pre-period where nothing happened,
refit, and the estimated effect must return near zero. It does (\cref{fig:sc:placebotime}): a fake
launch at week \nbSevenPlaceboTimeWeek{} returns \keur{\nbSevenPlaceboTimeGap} per week, inside the
pre-fit noise. A method that manufactured an effect at a placebo date could not be trusted at the real
one; this one does not.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.8\quad The randomization interval by inversion}
\label{app:sc:randint}
The placebo totals yield an \emph{interval}, not only a p-value, by inverting the test
(\cref{eq:sc:ri}). The placebo distribution is the spread of totals this estimator produces when the
true effect is zero; subtract its upper and lower percentiles from the point estimate and the result
is a 90\,\% interval that never assumed a shape for the errors but measured one, from units known to be
untreated. Here it is $[\keur{\nbSevenRiLo},\ \keur{\nbSevenRiHi}]$ and it contains the planted
\keur{\nbSevenTrueTotal}; \cref{fig:sc:inversion} draws the construction. Its width is honest
precisely because it committed to nothing about the
error's autocorrelation, which is what makes it the referee for the posterior in \cref{sec:sc:compare}.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.9\quad Which referee grades which posterior}
\label{app:sc:referee}

Grading an interval means asking how often it would cover across fresh draws of the world, so each
posterior must be weighed against the sampling variability of \emph{its own} estimator, and the
iid and autocorrelated fits are different estimators. \Cref{tab:sc:scales}'s referee column,
\keur{\nbSevenSeedSd} (EUR 000), is the seed-to-seed spread of the \emph{iid} arm's error, and it is
the right yardstick for the iid posterior. That posterior fails against it: the iid posterior's
\keur{\nbSevenIidSd} underprices that spread by \nbSevenUnderprice$\times$. The autocorrelated arm makes different errors, and its
own spread across the same \nbSevenNPanels{} fresh panels is \keur{\nbSevenSeedSdArone}. That wider
number, not \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd}, is the only referee entitled to judge the autocorrelated
posterior. Graded against it, the autocorrelated interval is close to calibrated rather than tight:
its coverage is \pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone} against a nominal ninety (\cref{fig:sc:coverage}), and
the small shortfall traces
to the arm's modest \nbSevenBiasArone{} (EUR 000) bias, not to a too-narrow interval. The trap the
single ``referee'' column of \cref{tab:sc:scales} invites is to grade both posteriors against the
iid arm's spread, which would wrongly flag the autocorrelated fit as narrow.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.10\quad The AR(1) likelihood as GLS re-weighting}
\label{app:sc:ar1}

Written in observables, the AR(1) residual law \cref{eq:sc:ar1} gives the likelihood
%
\begin{align}
  Y_{00} &\;\sim\; \mathcal N\!\Big(\mu_0,\ \tfrac{\sigma^{2}}{1 - \rho^{2}}\Big),
        \qquad
  Y_{0t} \;\sim\; \mathcal N\!\big(\mu_t + \rho\,(Y_{0,t-1} - \mu_{t-1}),\ \sigma^{2}\big),
        \quad t \ge 1, \label{eq:sc:ar1lik}
\end{align}
%
with $\mu_t = \sum_j w_j Y_{jt}$. The first line is the \emph{stationary initial condition}: at
$t = 0$ there is no earlier residual to condition on, so the marginal variance of a stationary
AR(1), $\sigma^{2}/(1-\rho^{2})$, stands in for $\sigma^{2}$. Dropping it makes the likelihood treat
the first week as if it carried no accumulated persistence, and biases $\rho$ upward.

An AR(1) likelihood is a generalised-least-squares re-weighting of the pre-period. Independent-error
least squares (\cref{eq:sc:slsqp}) weights every pre-period week equally; GLS with an AR(1)
covariance down-weights a week its neighbours already predict and up-weights a surprising one. That
changes which weeks the fit trusts, hence the donor weights, hence the counterfactual, hence the
point estimate, which is why the repaired estimate in \cref{sec:sc:compare} moves off the iid
number \keur{\nbSevenIidTotal} to \keur{\nbSevenBayesTotal}, beside the classical
\keur{\nbSevenClTotal} though by a different weight profile (\cref{fig:sc:weightprofiles}).
\Cref{fig:sc:glsweights} draws the re-weighting itself: the flat weight the iid likelihood gives
every pre-period week against the AR(1) profile at the posterior-mean $\rho$. The
identification is untouched; the estimator is not.
\input{build/floats/nb07_gls_weights}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.11\quad The autocorrelation of a persistent offset}
\label{app:sc:acf}

Under independence, the uncertainty of an $n$-week total scales as
%
\begin{equation}
  \sd\Big(\sum_{t \in \text{post}} e_t\Big) \;=\; \sqrt{n}\,\sigma
  \qquad \text{(iid errors)} .
  \label{eq:sc:sqrtn}
\end{equation}
%
But the blend's error is not iid. Whatever share of the treated market's factor loadings the weights
fail to match leaks the \emph{shared} factors straight into the gap,
%
\begin{equation}
  e_t \;\approx\; \big(\lambda_0 - \textstyle\sum_j w_j \lambda_j\big)^{\!\top}
        (g_t,\, s_t,\, m_t) \;+\; \varepsilon_{0t},
  \label{eq:sc:leak}
\end{equation}
%
and $m_t$ is a random walk (\cref{eq:sc:macro}). Over a \nbSevenNPost-week window a random walk
barely turns around, so the mismatch term is, to a good approximation, a \emph{constant}: one
draw of ``how badly did this donor pool match?'', held for the whole post-period. Write the error
as an offset plus noise, writing $\omega$ for the offset draw (a letter of its own, so it cannot be
mistaken for the campaign cost $c$ or a market baseline $c_i$):
%
\begin{equation}
  e_t \;=\; \omega \;+\; \varepsilon_t,
  \qquad \omega \sim (0,\ \sigma_\omega^{2}) \ \text{drawn once per panel},
  \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim (0,\ \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}) \ \text{fresh each week}.
  \label{eq:sc:offset}
\end{equation}
%
Two consequences follow. First, the autocorrelation is flat:
%
\begin{equation}
  \rho_k \;=\; \frac{\sigma_\omega^{2}}{\sigma_\omega^{2} + \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}}
  \qquad \textbf{for every lag } k,
  \label{eq:sc:flatrho}
\end{equation}
%
which does not decay. A short-memory process, an AR(1) with $|\rho| < 1$, \emph{dies out}
($\rho_k = \rho^k \to 0$); a persistent offset does not. So the question to ask of \cref{fig:sc:acf}
is not how large $\rho_1$ is but whether $\rho_8$ is still there. Second, the variance of the sum
picks up an $n^{2}$ term. In general
$\Var(\sum_1^n e_t) = \sigma^2\big(n + 2\sum_{k<n}(n-k)\rho_k\big)$; under \cref{eq:sc:offset} that
collapses to
%
\begin{equation}
  \Var\Big(\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t\Big) \;=\; n^{2}\,\sigma_\omega^{2} \;+\; n\,\sigma_\varepsilon^{2}.
  \label{eq:sc:n2}
\end{equation}
%
The $n^{2}$ term rides on $\sigma_\omega^{2}$, not on $\rho$ being large: a \emph{modest} autocorrelation
that refuses to decay is enough. Per-week bands need only the marginal variance
$\sigma_\omega^{2} + \sigma_\varepsilon^{2}$, which \cref{eq:sc:ppc} gets roughly right, which is why
they cover at \pc{\nbSevenWeekCov}. The total needs the covariances too, and \cref{eq:sc:lik} sets
every $\rho_k$ to zero, missing the entire $n^{2}$ term.

The decomposition confirms itself on a worked example. Splitting the placebo errors into their two
parts gives $\sigma_\omega = \nbSevenOffsetSd$ and $\sigma_\varepsilon = \nbSevenNoiseSd$, and
\cref{eq:sc:flatrho} then predicts an autocorrelation of \nbSevenRhoImplied{} at every lag, against
the \nbSevenRhoOne{} and \nbSevenRhoEight{} measured in \cref{fig:sc:acf}. Substituting the same two
numbers into \cref{eq:sc:n2} gives a true standard deviation of \keur{\nbSevenTotalSdTrue} for the
\nbSevenNPost-week total against the \keur{\nbSevenTotalSdIid} an iid likelihood charges, an
under-pricing of \nbSevenTotalSdRatio$\times$ from the error's structure alone. Measured directly
across fresh panels it is \nbSevenUnderprice$\times$ (\keur{\nbSevenSeedSd} against
\keur{\nbSevenIidSd}). The two agree on the factor; they agree only roughly on the level, since
\cref{eq:sc:offset} is a two-parameter caricature of the error and not a full account of it.

Why does the caricature sit \emph{below} the measured spread (\keur{\nbSevenTotalSdTrue} structural
against the seed-to-seed \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd})? Because treating the mismatch as a \emph{constant}
$\omega$ understates it. The mismatch is really $(\lambda_0 - \sum_j w_j \lambda_j)^{\!\top}(g_t, s_t, m_t)$,
and its worst-behaved piece is the macro random walk $m_t$, which is \emph{integrated}, not constant:
it drifts over the post-period, and the sum of an integrated series compounds faster than the sum of
a constant. Formally $\sum_{t=1}^{n} m_t = \sum_u (n - u + 1)\,\eta_u$ has variance
$\approx n^{3}\sigma_\eta^{2}/3$, an $n^{3}$ growth against the offset model's $n^{2}$. At
$n = \nbSevenNPost$ this is not the asymptotic regime, but the direction is exactly right: the true
total variance grows \emph{faster} than the constant-offset caricature admits, which is why the
structural \keur{\nbSevenTotalSdTrue} lands below the measured \keur{\nbSevenSeedSd}. The constant
offset is the leading, tractable term; the integrated remainder is why the honest number is larger,
and it is the same near-integrated structure that makes a stationary AR(1) (\cref{eq:sc:ar1}) a
convenient caricature of the error rather than its true law, one that matches the variance of the
sum without matching its flat autocorrelation. \Cref{fig:sc:growth} puts the three growth laws on
one axis, with the measured \nbSevenNPost-week point sitting between the offset and level curves,
which is the whole argument of \cref{sec:sc:sts} in one picture.
\input{build/floats/nb07_growth}

\begin{intuition}{Why the pre-period fit could never have warned you}
The obvious objection is that we fitted the weights on forty weeks of pre-period, so surely a
persistent error would have shown up there. It does not, and the reason is worth keeping. The
weights were chosen \emph{to make the pre-period gap small}: the fit absorbs, and thereby
whitens, most of the serial correlation it can see in the weeks it was shown. The persistence only
announces itself out of sample, in the weeks the fit was not allowed to touch. This is the same
lesson as an over-fitted regression's residuals looking beautifully white on the training set, and
it is why the diagnostic in \cref{fig:sc:acf} is run on \emph{placebo} panels rather than on the
fitted one. A residual you fitted to is not evidence.
\end{intuition}

The in-sample residual makes the point numerically. The pre-period residual of the fitted model
shows only mild autocorrelation: $\nbSevenAcfPreOne$, $\nbSevenAcfPreTwo$ and
$\nbSevenAcfPreThree$ at lags one to three, against a noise band of roughly $\pm\nbSevenAcfNoiseBand$.
It is positive throughout, but tame enough to shrug at, and only the first lag clears its own band. The fit
\emph{whitens} what it can see, because the weights were chosen to absorb precisely the in-sample
structure. Eyeballing the pre-fit residual would therefore have raised no serious alarm. The
persistence is a property of the \emph{out-of-sample} error, and only a design-based procedure
(one that refits the estimator on units known to be untreated) ever gets to look at it.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.12\quad Value of information}
\label{app:sc:voi}
\Cref{eq:sc:voi} prices the decision's remaining uncertainty. Acting now on the mean earns
$\max(\E[\text{profit}] - c,\, 0)$; acting \emph{after} the uncertainty resolves earns
$\E[\max(\text{profit} - c,\, 0)]$, because you would commit only in the worlds where profit clears
cost. The gap between the two is the value of perfect information, the most any study could be worth
(\cref{fig:sc:appvoi}). Here it is \keur{\nbSevenVoi}, small because the posterior sits close to the
cost line on the losing side, so perfect information would rarely overturn the hold the mean already
implies. Being an upper bound, it caps the confirmatory-research budget: a study costing more than
\keur{\nbSevenVoi} cannot be justified on this decision alone.
\input{build/floats/nb07_app_voi}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.13\quad Bernstein--von Mises}
\label{app:sc:bvm}
Why do the classical and Bayesian point estimates agree (\cref{sec:sc:compare})? The
Bernstein--von Mises theorem: with a well-specified likelihood and enough data, the posterior
concentrates around the maximum-likelihood estimate, so a credible interval and a confidence interval
converge. \Cref{fig:sc:appbvm} shows the \emph{centres} doing exactly that: the bootstrap sampling
distribution of the classical total (resample the pre-period, refit, repeat) is centred on the
Bayesian posterior mean. Their widths are in the same ballpark rather than identical, and, tellingly,
both are narrow, because both inherit the same iid error model and so under-price the total in the
same direction (\cref{sec:sc:compare}). The two paradigms answer different questions yet return the
same estimate. Where they part is not the estimate but the interval \emph{width} once the likelihood
is repaired (\cref{sec:sc:compare}) and the ability to state $\Prob(\text{profit} > c)$ at all
(\cref{sec:sc:euro}).
\input{build/floats/nb07_app_bvm}

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.14\quad The margin trap: break-even algebra}
\label{app:sc:margin}
Advertising buys revenue; the firm keeps only its gross margin $m$ on it, so the profit a lift
$\tau$ earns is $m\tau$. The campaign pays exactly when $m\tau > c$, and two equivalent thresholds
follow: a \emph{break-even lift} $b = c/m$, and a \emph{break-even iROAS} of
$\text{iROAS} = \tau/c > 1/m$. Worked on this campaign: at $m = \pc{\nbSevenMargin}$ and
$c = \keur{\nbSevenCost}$ the break-even lift is \keur{\nbSevenBreakevenLift} and the break-even
iROAS is \nbSevenBreakevenIroas{}. The estimated lift \keur{\nbSevenClTotal} converts to
\keur{\nbSevenClProfit} of profit and a net of \keur{\nbSevenClNet}; \cref{fig:sc:marginfunnel}
draws the whole waterfall. The trap is reading the gross \nbSevenIroas{} as the return: the number
that decides anything is the net after margin, and it is two orders of magnitude smaller than the
apparent \keur{\nbSevenApparentProfit} win.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.15\quad The matched-pairs estimator and its standard error}
\label{app:sc:pairs}
The randomized benchmark of \cref{sec:sc:real} averages one number per pair: the pair difference
$d_p$ of \cref{eq:sc:did}, the treated geo's pre-to-post change minus its size-matched partner's.
Randomization happened at the geo level \emph{within pairs}, so the pair differences are the
design's own unit of variability, and the standard error is built from their scatter,
$NT \cdot s_d / \sqrt{P}$: no model, no likelihood. Two properties do the work. First, pairing is
variance reduction: the geo-size factor differences out within each pair, and ignoring the pairs
inflates the standard error from \kusd{\nbSevenBDidSe} to \kusd{\nbSevenBUnpairedSe}, a factor of
\nbSevenBPairingGain{} (\cref{fig:sc:realpairs}). Second, treating geo-days as the unit instead
would count thousands of correlated observations as independent, the \citet{bertrand2004} error in
another costume. The scatter of the $d_p$, not any fitted curve, is what ``unbiased by
construction'' looks like on the page.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.16\quad The effective donor count}
\label{app:sc:effdonors}
A weight vector on \nbSevenNDonors{} donors can act like a blend of three markets or of thirty, and
the \emph{effective donor count} $1/\sum_j w_j^{2}$ (the inverse Herfindahl index) measures which.
It equals $J$ when the weight is spread evenly over $J$ donors and $1$ when a single donor carries
everything. Worked here: the classical fit concentrates its blend on \nbSevenClEffDonors{}
effective donors, the flat-Dirichlet posterior spreads the same reconstruction over
\nbSevenBayesEffDonors{} (\cref{fig:sc:weightprofiles}, \cref{tab:sc:weights}). The number is a
readability statistic for the estimator itself: a small count reads as a nameable portfolio of
markets, a large one as a diffuse average, and \cref{sec:sc:model} prices that trade.

\phantomsection
\subsection*{\thechapter.A.17\quad The Monte-Carlo error of a coverage estimate}
\label{app:sc:mcerr}
A coverage rate measured on $n$ fresh panels is a binomial proportion, and it carries sampling
error of its own: $\widehat{\text{se}} = \sqrt{\hat p\,(1-\hat p)/n}$. On the
$n = \nbSevenNPanels$ panels of \cref{sec:sc:valid} that is \nbSevenCovSeIid{} percentage points at
the iid arm's \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} and \nbSevenCovSeArone{} points at the repaired arm's
\pc{\nbSevenTotalCovArone} (\nbSevenCovSeRi{} at the design-based \pc{\nbSevenRiCov}). The
conclusion this arithmetic protects: the iid arm's \pc{\nbSevenTotalCov} sits many standard errors
below the nominal ninety and is broken by any reckoning, while the repaired and design-based rates
are within about one standard error of ninety and of each other, indistinguishable at this
resolution (\cref{fig:sc:coverage}). Quoting their digits as a ranking would be reading noise.

  The analysis of \crefrange{sec:sc:dgm}{sec:sc:wrong} is
  \code{notebooks/07\_geo\_lift\_synthetic\_control.ipynb};
  \cref{sec:sc:real} is \code{notebooks/07b\_geo\_lift\_real\_data.ipynb}, which loads Google's
  published geo-experiment panel \citep{kerman2017} directly. Both run in a fast teaching mode
  (\code{CMP\_FAST=1}, short chains) and a full mode (\code{CMP\_FAST=0}); every number in this
  chapter comes from the full run, emitted by \code{cmp.report} and injected here as a macro. The
  simulator is \code{cmp.dgp.geo\_panel}, the estimators are \code{cmp.estimators}, and the seeds
  are fixed in the notebooks. \citeauthor{abadie2010}'s California Proposition~99 study remains the
  canonical public panel on which to practise the method \citep{abadie2010}.}
